title: Wales

List   * 46.5% [no religion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Wales "Irreligion in Wales") * 43.6% [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Wales "Christianity in Wales") * 2.2% [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Wales "Islam in Wales") * 0.4% [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Wales "Hinduism in Wales") * 0.3% [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Wales "Buddhism in Wales") * 0.1% [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism_in_Wales "Sikhism in Wales") * 0.1% [Judaism](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Wales "History of the Jews in Wales") * 0.5% [other](/wiki/Religion_in_Wales "Religion in Wales") * 6.3% not stated

infobox.value: None officially;[[1]](#cite_note-1)   "*[Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau](/wiki/Hen_Wlad_Fy_Nhadau "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau")*" (*de facto*)   ("Land of My Fathers") | |

([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *[Cymru](/wiki/Cymru "Cymru")* [[ˈkəmrɨ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Welsh "Help:IPA/Welsh") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Cymru.ogg "File:Cymru.ogg")) is a [country](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") that is part of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). Located on the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), it is bordered by the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") to the north and west, [England](/wiki/England "England") to the [east](/wiki/England%E2%80%93Wales_border "England–Wales border"), the [Bristol Channel](/wiki/Bristol_Channel "Bristol Channel") to the south, and the [Celtic Sea](/wiki/Celtic_Sea "Celtic Sea") to the south-west. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wales&action=edit), it had a population of 3.2 million.[[2]](#cite_note-2021_Nomis-2) It has a total area of 21,218 square kilometres (8,192 sq mi)[[8]](#cite_note-ONS_Standard_Area_Measurement-11) and over 2,700 kilometres (1,680 mi) of [coastline](/wiki/Coastline_of_Wales "Coastline of Wales").[[13]](#cite_note-Coastline-17) It is largely mountainous with its higher peaks in the north and central areas, including [Snowdon](/wiki/Snowdon "Snowdon") (*Yr Wyddfa*), its highest summit.[[14]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-18) The country lies within the [north temperate zone](/wiki/North_temperate_zone "North temperate zone") and has a changeable, [maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate"). Its capital and largest city is [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff").

**Wales** ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *[Cymru](/wiki/Cymru "Cymru")* [[ˈkəmrɨ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Welsh "Help:IPA/Welsh") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Cymru.ogg "File:Cymru.ogg")) is a [country](/wiki/Countries_of_the_United_Kingdom "Countries of the United Kingdom") that is part of the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"). Located on the island of [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), it is bordered by the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") to the north and west, [England](/wiki/England "England") to the [east](/wiki/England%E2%80%93Wales_border "England–Wales border"), the [Bristol Channel](/wiki/Bristol_Channel "Bristol Channel") to the south, and the [Celtic Sea](/wiki/Celtic_Sea "Celtic Sea") to the south-west. As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Wales&action=edit), it had a population of 3.2 million.[[2]](#cite_note-2021_Nomis-2) It has a total area of 21,218 square kilometres (8,192 sq mi)[[8]](#cite_note-ONS_Standard_Area_Measurement-11) and over 2,700 kilometres (1,680 mi) of [coastline](/wiki/Coastline_of_Wales "Coastline of Wales").[[13]](#cite_note-Coastline-17) It is largely mountainous with its higher peaks in the north and central areas, including [Snowdon](/wiki/Snowdon "Snowdon") (*Yr Wyddfa*), its highest summit.[[14]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-18) The country lies within the [north temperate zone](/wiki/North_temperate_zone "North temperate zone") and has a changeable, [maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate"). Its capital and largest city is [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff").

A distinct [Welsh culture](/wiki/Welsh_culture "Welsh culture") emerged among the [Celtic Britons](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons") after the [Roman withdrawal from Britain](/wiki/Roman_withdrawal_from_Britain "Roman withdrawal from Britain") in the 5th century, and Wales was briefly united under [Gruffudd ap Llywelyn](/wiki/Gruffudd_ap_Llywelyn "Gruffudd ap Llywelyn") in 1055. After over 200 years of war, the [conquest of Wales](/wiki/Conquest_of_Wales "Conquest of Wales") was completed by King [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") in 1283, though [Owain Glyndŵr](/wiki/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r "Owain Glyndŵr") led the [Welsh Revolt](/wiki/Welsh_Revolt "Welsh Revolt") against [English rule](/wiki/Wales_in_the_Late_Middle_Ages "Wales in the Late Middle Ages") in the early 15th century, and briefly re-established an independent Welsh state with its own national parliament ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *senedd*). In the 16th century the whole of Wales was [annexed](/wiki/Annexed "Annexed") by England and incorporated within the [English legal system](/wiki/English_legal_system "English legal system") under the [Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535_and_1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542"). Distinctive [Welsh politics](/wiki/Welsh_politics "Welsh politics") developed in the 19th century. [Welsh Liberalism](/wiki/Welsh_Liberal_Party "Welsh Liberal Party"), exemplified in the late 19th and early 20th century by [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George"), was displaced by the growth of [socialism](/wiki/Socialism "Socialism") and the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)"). Welsh national feeling grew over the century: a [nationalist](/wiki/Nationalist "Nationalist") party, [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru"), was formed in 1925, and the [Welsh Language Society](/wiki/Welsh_Language_Society "Welsh Language Society") in 1962. A governing system of [Welsh devolution](/wiki/Welsh_devolution "Welsh devolution") is employed in Wales, of which the most major step was the formation of the [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd") (Welsh Parliament, formerly the National Assembly for Wales) in 1998, responsible for a range of [devolved policy matters](/wiki/Welsh_law "Welsh law").

At the dawn of the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution_in_Wales "Industrial Revolution in Wales"), development of the [mining](/wiki/Mining_in_Wales "Mining in Wales") and [metallurgical](/wiki/Metallurgical "Metallurgical") industries transformed the country from an [agricultural society](/wiki/Agrarian_society "Agrarian society") into an [industrial](/wiki/Industrial_society "Industrial society") one; the [South Wales Coalfield](/wiki/South_Wales_Coalfield "South Wales Coalfield")'s exploitation caused a rapid expansion of Wales's population. Two-thirds of the population live in [South Wales](/wiki/South_Wales "South Wales"), including Cardiff, [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea"), [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), and the [nearby valleys](/wiki/South_Wales_Valleys "South Wales Valleys"). The [eastern region](/wiki/North_East_Wales "North East Wales") of [North Wales](/wiki/North_Wales "North Wales") has about a sixth of the overall population, with [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham "Wrexham") being the largest northern city. The remaining parts of Wales are [sparsely populated](/wiki/Sparsely_populated "Sparsely populated"). Since decline of the country's traditional extractive and heavy industries, the [public sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector"), light and service industries, and [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_Wales "Tourism in Wales") play major roles in its [economy](/wiki/Economy_of_Wales "Economy of Wales"). [Agriculture in Wales](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Wales "Agriculture in Wales") is largely livestock-based, making Wales a net exporter of animal produce, contributing towards national [agricultural self-sufficiency](/wiki/Agricultural_self-sufficiency "Agricultural self-sufficiency").

Both [Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") and English are [official languages](/wiki/Official_language "Official language"). A majority of the population of Wales speaks English. Welsh is the dominant language in parts of the [north](/wiki/North_Wales "North Wales") and [west](/wiki/West_Wales "West Wales"), with a total of 538,300 [Welsh speakers](/wiki/Welsh_speakers "Welsh speakers") across the entire country. Wales has [four UNESCO world heritage sites](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Wales "List of World Heritage Sites in Wales"), of which three are in the north.

## Etymology

Main articles: [Etymology of Wales](/wiki/Etymology_of_Wales "Etymology of Wales") and [Cymru](/wiki/Cymru "Cymru")

The English words *Wales* and *Welsh* derive from the same [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") root (singular *[Wealh](/wiki/Wealh "Wealh")*, plural *Wēalas*), a descendant of [Proto-Germanic](/wiki/Proto-Germanic "Proto-Germanic") \**[Walhaz](/wiki/Walhaz "Walhaz")*, which was itself derived from the name of the [Gauls](/wiki/Gauls "Gauls") known to the Romans as *[Volcae](/wiki/Volcae "Volcae")*. This term was later used to refer indiscriminately to inhabitants of the [Western Roman Empire](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire").[[15]](#cite_note-19) [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") came to use the term to refer to the [Britons](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons") in particular; the plural form *Wēalas* evolved into the name for their territory, Wales.[[16]](#cite_note-Wales_Hist_71-20)[[17]](#cite_note-Tolkien_1-21) Historically in [Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), the words were not restricted to modern Wales or to the Welsh but were used to refer to anything that Anglo-Saxons associated with Britons, including other non-Germanic territories in Britain (e.g. [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall")) and places in Anglo-Saxon territory associated with Britons (e.g. [Walworth](/wiki/Walworth,_County_Durham "Walworth, County Durham") in County Durham and [Walton](/wiki/Walton,_Leeds "Walton, Leeds") in West Yorkshire).[[18]](#cite_note-22)

The modern Welsh name for themselves is *Cymry*, and *[Cymru](/wiki/Cymru "Cymru")* is the Welsh name for Wales. These words (both of which are pronounced [[ˈkəm.rɨ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Welsh "Help:IPA/Welsh")) are descended from the [Brythonic](/wiki/Common_Brittonic "Common Brittonic") word *combrogi*, meaning 'fellow-countrymen',[[19]](#cite_note-Hist_Wales_69-23)[[20]](#cite_note-24) and probably came into use before the 7th century.[[21]](#cite_note-25) In literature, they could be spelt *Kymry* or *Cymry*, regardless of whether it referred to the people or their homeland.[[19]](#cite_note-Hist_Wales_69-23) The [Latinised](/wiki/Romanization_(cultural) "Romanization (cultural)") forms of these names, *Cambrian*, *Cambric* and *[Cambria](/wiki/Cambria "Cambria")*, survive as names such as the [Cambrian Mountains](/wiki/Cambrian_Mountains "Cambrian Mountains") and the [Cambrian](/wiki/Cambrian "Cambrian") geological period.[[22]](#cite_note-26)

## History

Main articles: [History of Wales](/wiki/History_of_Wales "History of Wales") and [Timeline of Welsh history](/wiki/Timeline_of_Welsh_history "Timeline of Welsh history")

See also: [Archaeology of Wales](/wiki/Archaeology_of_Wales "Archaeology of Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/Caradog_%285227657%29.jpg/250px-Caradog_%285227657%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Caradog_(5227657).jpg)

[Caradog](/wiki/Caratacus "Caratacus") (depicted by Thomas Prydderch), leader of the north Wales Celtic tribe the [Ordovices](/wiki/Ordovices "Ordovices").

Although the Welsh nation did not arise until the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the territory of Wales was permanently settled from the end of the last ice age onwards.[[23]](#cite_note-CA_2007-27) These first farmers left many impressive funerary monuments, as well as settlement sites that speak to a dispersed culture. With the arrival of the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age"), the [Great Orme](/wiki/Great_Orme "Great Orme") in North Wales became Britain's premier producer of copper, one of the key ores for smelting bronze.[[24]](#cite_note-28) It is likely that the wealth of mineral resources in Britain, and especially Wales, attracted the Roman invasion,[[25]](#cite_note-29) but by this time the island had become distinctively Celtic in culture, and the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") population was largely replaced. It was this [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age") Celtic culture, and their common language, that were called the Britons by the Romans.[[26]](#cite_note-30): 90

With the departure of the Romans, Britain fractured into various kingdoms. Despite this, there is a sense in which the Roman withdrawal of 383 created a post-Roman nation of Britons, with [Magnus Maximus](/wiki/Magnus_Maximus "Magnus Maximus") proclaimed Roman emperor in Britannia and Gaul. Although long before the term *Cymry* (the Welsh term for the Welsh) had been adopted, the concept of a British people, from which the Welsh would emerge, was created here.[[27]](#cite_note-Davies_1994-31): 54

Encroachment by Germanic [Anglo-Saxon settlers](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon_settlers "Anglo-Saxon settlers") gradually displaced the indigenous culture and language of the Britons, and one group of these Britons became isolated by the geography of the western peninsula, bounded by the sea and English neighbours. It was these English neighbours who named the land Wallia, and the people Welsh.[[28]](#cite_note-Johnes_2019-32): 15

The people of Wallia, medieval Wales, remained divided into separate kingdoms that fought with each other as much as they fought their English neighbours.[[28]](#cite_note-Johnes_2019-32): 15 Neither were the communities homogeneously Welsh. Place name, historical records and archaeological evidence point to coastal Viking/Norse settlement in places such as Swansea, Fishguard and Anglesey,[[29]](#cite_note-Redknap_2000-33): 4, 13 and Saxons settled inland amongst the Welsh in places such as Presteigne.[[30]](#cite_note-34): 125

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Laws_of_Hywel_Dda_%28f.1.v%29_King_Hywel_cropped.jpg/250px-Laws_of_Hywel_Dda_%28f.1.v%29_King_Hywel_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laws_of_Hywel_Dda_(f.1.v)_King_Hywel_cropped.jpg)

[Hywel Dda](/wiki/Hywel_Dda "Hywel Dda") enthroned

In the 10th century, Hywel ap Cadell, later known as [Hywel Dda](/wiki/Hywel_Dda "Hywel Dda"), formed the kingdom of Deheubarth from inheritances in Dyfed and Seisyllwg, and then gained control of the kingdoms of Gwynedd and Powys in 942.[[31]](#cite_note-Lloyd_1912-35): 337–338 With control of nearly all the territory of Wales,[[28]](#cite_note-Johnes_2019-32): 22 he codified Welsh law, a law code that survived the later fracture of his kingdom, and that became a significant step in the creation of the nation.[[28]](#cite_note-Johnes_2019-32): 29 With a common culture and an external threat, the kingdoms of Wales began to see themselves as one people.[[28]](#cite_note-Johnes_2019-32): 30

A century later the Kingdom of Gwynedd was in ascendency, and Gruffydd ap Llywelyn subdued all opposition by 1057, becoming the only king to unite all of Wales, and parts of England on the border. "Thus, from about 1057 until his death in 1063, the whole of Wales recognised the kingship of Gruffydd ap Llywelyn. For about seven brief years, Wales was one, under one ruler, a feat with neither precedent nor successor."[[27]](#cite_note-Davies_1994-31): 100

The kingdom did not last, and Gruffydd met his death as a result of a surprise attack by Tostig, brother of the English King, Harold.[[32]](#cite_note-DNB1-36): 305–307 After Gruffydd's death, Harold married his widow, but she would be widowed again by the Norman invasion of England in 1066.[[33]](#cite_note-DNB2-37)

The Normans followed their invasion of England with incursions into Wales, forming the semi-independent Norman [Welsh marches](/wiki/Welsh_marches "Welsh marches") (from the French for borderlands), and dividing them from the unconquered Pura Wallia.[[28]](#cite_note-Johnes_2019-32): 25 The fortunes of Welsh marcher lords and various Welsh princes ebbed and flowed, until Llywelyn ab Iorwerth (Llywelyn the Great) forced all other Welsh princes to submit to him in 1216.[[34]](#cite_note-Pierce_1959b-38) Yet Wales was divided again after his death, and it was left for his grandson Llywelyn ap Gruffudd to secure the supremacy once more, recognised as Prince of Wales by the English king, Henry III, in the treaty of Montgomery of 1267.[[35]](#cite_note-Pierce_1959a-39)

Relations with Henry's successor, Edward I, broke down and led to a war of conquest, concluding in 1283 with English victory.[[36]](#cite_note-Carpenter_2003-40): 510 The following year the statute of Rhuddlan ended Welsh independence. Wales was divided between principality, ruled by Edward; and the marches, ruled by feudal marcher lords.[[37]](#cite_note-Davies_2000-41): 461 This persisted, despite the [Welsh rebellion](/wiki/Glynd%C5%B5r_rebellion "Glyndŵr rebellion") under Owain Glyndŵr of 1400–1415,[[38]](#cite_note-42) until the rise of the Tudors, with Welsh support.[[39]](#cite_note-43): 274 With the [Laws in Wales Acts](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts "Laws in Wales Acts") of Henry VIII, the Welsh became full citizens in the Kingdom of England, with parliamentary representation.[[40]](#cite_note-44): 274 The Welsh border was also formally defined and the territory reunited.[[41]](#cite_note-45): 268–273

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/George_Childs_Dowlais_Ironworks_1840.jpg/250px-George_Childs_Dowlais_Ironworks_1840.jpg)](/wiki/File:George_Childs_Dowlais_Ironworks_1840.jpg)

*Dowlais Ironworks* (1840) by George Childs (1798–1875)

The [Act of Union 1707](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1707 "Act of Union 1707") created the Kingdom of Great Britain.[[42]](#cite_note-Act_of_Union_1707-46) The industrial revolution and the beginning of empire led to the rapid increase in mining and exploitation of Welsh natural materials – metals, coal and slate. The population of Wales expanded rapidly[[43]](#cite_note-Williams_1985-47): 185 and Wales moved to the centre of the British economy, but the changes bred resentment, this time towards industrialists and not the English state.[[28]](#cite_note-Johnes_2019-32): 61 Meanwhile, a series of religious revivals transformed the character of the nation, beginning a tradition of non-conformism.[[44]](#cite_note-48): 345–50 This carried over into the political sphere too. The rapid industrialisation of parts of Wales gave rise to strong and [radical](/wiki/Radicalism_(historical) "Radicalism (historical)") Welsh [working class](/wiki/Working_class "Working class") movements which led to the [Merthyr Rising of 1831](/wiki/Merthyr_Rising_1831 "Merthyr Rising 1831"), the widespread support for [Chartism](/wiki/Chartism "Chartism"), and the [Newport Rising](/wiki/Newport_Rising "Newport Rising") of 1839.[[45]](#cite_note-49) Strong liberal traditions were forged and later replaced by socialism.[[46]](#cite_note-50): 34 Since 1922 Wales has voted Labour in every general election.[[47]](#cite_note-51)

From the mid 19th century until 1914, Wales experienced a strengthened political culture, religious and cultural revival, renewed interest in Welsh literature, the revival of [eisteddfodau](/wiki/Eisteddfodau "Eisteddfodau"). There was a thriving economy, a renewed interest in Welsh language, and music, non-conformist Christianity and the emergence of strong national identity, along with the founding of many national institutions.[[48]](#cite_note-52): 95 However, the period also saw the publication of a report on education that became known as the [Treachery of the Blue Books](/wiki/Treachery_of_the_Blue_Books "Treachery of the Blue Books"). The report blamed Welsh language and non-conformism for poor educational standards.[[49]](#cite_note-53): 2 This fed the rise of the Welsh nationalist movement, expressed in the [Cymru Fydd](/wiki/Cymru_Fydd "Cymru Fydd") movement, which advocated for greater autonomy and recognition of Welsh identity within the United Kingdom.[[50]](#cite_note-54) Calls for [devolution](/wiki/Welsh_devolution "Welsh devolution") grew over the course of a century, and the [Government of Wales Act 1998](/wiki/Government_of_Wales_Act_1998 "Government of Wales Act 1998") created a devolved Welsh assembly for the first time, now renamed the Senedd or Welsh Parliament.[[51]](#cite_note-55)

## Geography

Main article: [Geography of Wales](/wiki/Geography_of_Wales "Geography of Wales")

See also: [List of settlements in Wales by population](/wiki/List_of_settlements_in_Wales_by_population "List of settlements in Wales by population"), [List of towns in Wales](/wiki/List_of_towns_in_Wales "List of towns in Wales"), and [List of cities in Wales](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Wales "List of cities in Wales")

See also: [Natural resources of Wales](/wiki/Natural_resources_of_Wales "Natural resources of Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Yr_Wyddfa_%28Snowdon%29_from_Crib_Goch%2C_Parc_Cenedlaethol_Eryri_National_Park%2C_Cymru_%28Wales%29_09.jpg/250px-Yr_Wyddfa_%28Snowdon%29_from_Crib_Goch%2C_Parc_Cenedlaethol_Eryri_National_Park%2C_Cymru_%28Wales%29_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yr_Wyddfa_(Snowdon)_from_Crib_Goch,_Parc_Cenedlaethol_Eryri_National_Park,_Cymru_(Wales)_09.jpg)

[Snowdon](/wiki/Snowdon "Snowdon") ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Yr Wyddfa*) [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd "Gwynedd"), the highest mountain in Wales

Wales is a generally mountainous country on the western side of central southern Great Britain.[[52]](#cite_note-ONS_Cymru-56) It is about 170 miles (270 km) north to south.[[53]](#cite_note-57) The oft-quoted "[size of Wales](/wiki/The_size_of_Wales "The size of Wales")" is about 20,779 km2 (8,023 sq mi).[[54]](#cite_note-58) Wales is bordered by England to the east and by sea in all other directions: the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") to the north and west, [St George's Channel](/wiki/St_George%27s_Channel "St George's Channel") and the [Celtic Sea](/wiki/Celtic_Sea "Celtic Sea") to the southwest and the [Bristol Channel](/wiki/Bristol_Channel "Bristol Channel") to the south.[[55]](#cite_note-59)[[56]](#cite_note-60) Wales has about 1,680 miles (2,700 km) of coastline (along the mean high water mark), including the mainland, [Anglesey](/wiki/Anglesey "Anglesey") and [Holyhead](/wiki/Holy_Island,_Anglesey "Holy Island, Anglesey").[[13]](#cite_note-Coastline-17) [Over 50 islands](/wiki/List_of_islands_of_Wales "List of islands of Wales") lie off the Welsh mainland, the largest being Anglesey, in the north-west.[[57]](#cite_note-61)

Much of Wales's diverse landscape is mountainous, particularly in the north and central regions. The mountains were shaped during the last ice age, the [Devensian glaciation](/wiki/Devensian_glaciation "Devensian glaciation"). The highest mountains in Wales are in [Snowdonia](/wiki/Snowdonia "Snowdonia") (*Eryri*), of which five are over 1,000 m (3,300 ft). The highest of these is [Snowdon](/wiki/Snowdon "Snowdon") (*Yr Wyddfa*), at 1,085 m (3,560 ft).[[58]](#cite_note-62)[[59]](#cite_note-WalesOnline_mynyddoedd-63) The 14 Welsh mountains, or 15 if including [Carnedd Gwenllian](/wiki/Carnedd_Gwenllian "Carnedd Gwenllian") – often discounted because of its low [topographic prominence](/wiki/Topographic_prominence "Topographic prominence") – over 3,000 feet (910 metres) high are known collectively as the [Welsh 3000s](/wiki/Welsh_3000s "Welsh 3000s") and are located in a small area in the north-west.[[60]](#cite_note-64) The highest outside the 3000s is [Aran Fawddwy](/wiki/Aran_Fawddwy "Aran Fawddwy"), at 905 metres (2,969 feet), in the south of Snowdonia.[[61]](#cite_note-65) The [Brecon Beacons](/wiki/Brecon_Beacons "Brecon Beacons") (*Bannau Brycheiniog*) are in the south (highest point [Pen y Fan](/wiki/Pen_y_Fan "Pen y Fan"), at 886 metres (2,907 feet)),[[62]](#cite_note-66) and are joined by the [Cambrian Mountains](/wiki/Cambrian_Mountains "Cambrian Mountains") in [Mid Wales](/wiki/Mid_Wales "Mid Wales") (highest point [Pumlumon](/wiki/Pumlumon "Pumlumon"), at 752 metres (2,467 feet)).[[63]](#cite_note-67)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Map_of_Wales.svg/250px-Map_of_Wales.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Wales.svg)

Relief map of Wales:  

Topography above 600 feet (180 m)

[National Parks](/wiki/National_parks_of_Wales "National parks of Wales")

Wales has [three national parks](/wiki/National_parks_of_Wales "National parks of Wales"): Snowdonia, Brecon Beacons, and [Pembrokeshire Coast](/wiki/Pembrokeshire_Coast "Pembrokeshire Coast") (*Arfordir Penfro*). It has [five Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Areas_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty_in_Wales "Areas of Outstanding Natural Beauty in Wales"): Anglesey, the [Clwydian Range and Dee Valley](/wiki/Clwydian_Range_and_Dee_Valley "Clwydian Range and Dee Valley"), the [Gower Peninsula](/wiki/Gower_Peninsula "Gower Peninsula"), the [Llŷn Peninsula](/wiki/Ll%C5%B7n_Peninsula "Llŷn Peninsula"), and the [Wye Valley](/wiki/Wye_Valley "Wye Valley").[[64]](#cite_note-68) The Gower Peninsula was the first area in the United Kingdom to be designated as an [Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty](/wiki/Area_of_Outstanding_Natural_Beauty "Area of Outstanding Natural Beauty"), in 1956. As of 2019, the [coastline of Wales](/wiki/Coastline_of_Wales "Coastline of Wales") had 40 [Blue Flag beaches](/wiki/Blue_Flag_beach "Blue Flag beach"), three Blue Flag marinas and one Blue Flag boat operator.[[65]](#cite_note-69) The south and west coasts of Wales, along with the Irish and Cornish coasts, are frequently blasted by Atlantic [westerlies](/wiki/Westerlies "Westerlies")/south-westerlies that, over the years, have sunk and wrecked many vessels. In 1859 over 110 ships were destroyed off the coast of Wales in a hurricane that saw more than 800 lives lost across Britain.[[66]](#cite_note-70) The greatest single loss occurred with the sinking of the *[Royal Charter](/wiki/Royal_Charter_(ship) "Royal Charter (ship)")* off Anglesey in which 459 people died.[[67]](#cite_note-71) The 19th century saw over 100 vessels lost with an average loss of 78 sailors per year.[[68]](#cite_note-Davies_814-72) Wartime action caused losses near Holyhead, [Milford Haven](/wiki/Milford_Haven "Milford Haven") and Swansea.[[68]](#cite_note-Davies_814-72) Because of offshore rocks and unlit islands, Anglesey and Pembrokeshire are still notorious for shipwrecks, most notably the [*Sea Empress* oil spill](/wiki/Sea_Empress_oil_spill "Sea Empress oil spill") in 1996.[[69]](#cite_note-73)

The first border between Wales and England was zonal, apart from around the River Wye, which was the first accepted boundary.[[70]](#cite_note-Davies75-74) Offa's Dyke was supposed to form an early distinct line but this was thwarted by Gruffudd ap Llewellyn, who reclaimed swathes of land beyond the dyke.[[70]](#cite_note-Davies75-74) The [Act of Union 1536](/wiki/Act_of_Union_1536 "Act of Union 1536") formed a linear border stretching from the mouth of the [Dee](/wiki/River_Dee,_Wales "River Dee, Wales") to the mouth of the Wye.[[70]](#cite_note-Davies75-74) Even after the Act of Union, many of the borders remained vague and moveable until the [Sunday Closing (Wales) Act 1881](/wiki/Sunday_Closing_(Wales)_Act_1881 "Sunday Closing (Wales) Act 1881"), which forced local businesses to decide which country they fell within to accept either the Welsh or English law.[[70]](#cite_note-Davies75-74)

### Geology

Main article: [Geology of Wales](/wiki/Geology_of_Wales "Geology of Wales")

The earliest geological period of the [Palaeozoic](/wiki/Palaeozoic "Palaeozoic") era, the [Cambrian](/wiki/Cambrian "Cambrian"), takes its name from the [Cambrian Mountains](/wiki/Cambrian_Mountains "Cambrian Mountains"), where geologists first identified Cambrian remnants.[[71]](#cite_note-75)[[72]](#cite_note-Sedgwick1852-76) In the mid-19th century, [Roderick Murchison](/wiki/Roderick_Murchison "Roderick Murchison") and [Adam Sedgwick](/wiki/Adam_Sedgwick "Adam Sedgwick") used their studies of Welsh geology to establish certain principles of [stratigraphy](/wiki/Stratigraphy "Stratigraphy") and [palaeontology](/wiki/Palaeontology "Palaeontology"). The next two periods of the Palaeozoic era, the [Ordovician](/wiki/Ordovician "Ordovician") and [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian"), were named after ancient Celtic tribes from this area.[[73]](#cite_note-77)[[74]](#cite_note-78)

A [Red kite](/wiki/Red_kite "Red kite"), considered one of the [national symbols of Wales](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Wales "National symbols of Wales") and voted the nation's favourite bird[[75]](#cite_note-Wales-2019-79)

Wales lies within the [north temperate zone](/wiki/North_temperate_zone "North temperate zone"). It has a changeable, [maritime climate](/wiki/Maritime_climate "Maritime climate") and is one of the wettest countries in Europe.[[76]](#cite_note-Met_Off_1-80)[[77]](#cite_note-Davies148-150-81) Welsh weather is often cloudy, wet and windy, with warm summers and mild winters.[[76]](#cite_note-Met_Off_1-80)[[78]](#cite_note-WalesOnline_soggy-82)

* Highest maximum temperature: 37.1 °C (99 °F) at [Hawarden](/wiki/Hawarden "Hawarden"), Flintshire on 18 July 2022.[[79]](#cite_note-83)
* Lowest minimum temperature: −23.3 °C (−10 °F) at [Rhayader](/wiki/Rhayader "Rhayader"), Radnorshire (now [Powys](/wiki/Powys "Powys")) on 21 January 1940.[[80]](#cite_note-Met_Office_Temp-84)
* Maximum number of hours of sunshine in a month: 354.3 hours at [Dale Fort](/wiki/Dale_Fort "Dale Fort"), Pembrokeshire in July 1955.[[80]](#cite_note-Met_Office_Temp-84)
* Minimum number of hours of sunshine in a month: 2.7 hours at Llwynon, [Brecknockshire](/wiki/Brecknockshire "Brecknockshire") in January 1962.[[80]](#cite_note-Met_Office_Temp-84)
* Maximum rainfall in a day (0900 UTC – 0900 UTC): 211 millimetres (8.3 in) at [Rhondda](/wiki/Rhondda "Rhondda"), Glamorgan, on 11 November 1929.[[81]](#cite_note-85)
* Wettest spot – an average of 4,473 millimetres (176 in) rain a year at [Crib Goch](/wiki/Crib_Goch "Crib Goch") in Snowdonia, Gwynedd (making it also the wettest spot in the United Kingdom).[[82]](#cite_note-86)

### Flora and fauna

Main article: [Biodiversity of Wales](/wiki/Biodiversity_of_Wales "Biodiversity of Wales")

Wales's wildlife is typical of Britain with several distinctions. Because of its long coastline, Wales hosts a variety of seabirds. The coasts and surrounding islands are home to colonies of [gannets](/wiki/Northern_gannet "Northern gannet"), [Manx shearwater](/wiki/Manx_shearwater "Manx shearwater"), [puffins](/wiki/Atlantic_puffin "Atlantic puffin"), [kittiwakes](/wiki/Black-legged_kittiwake "Black-legged kittiwake"), [shags](/wiki/European_shag "European shag") and [razorbills](/wiki/Razorbill "Razorbill"). In comparison, with 60 per cent of Wales above the 150m contour, the country also supports a variety of upland-habitat birds, including [raven](/wiki/Common_raven "Common raven") and [ring ouzel](/wiki/Ring_ouzel "Ring ouzel").[[83]](#cite_note-87)[[84]](#cite_note-88) [Birds of prey](/wiki/Birds_of_prey "Birds of prey") include the [merlin](/wiki/Merlin_(bird) "Merlin (bird)"), [hen harrier](/wiki/Hen_harrier "Hen harrier") and the [red kite](/wiki/Red_kite "Red kite"), a national symbol of Welsh wildlife.[[85]](#cite_note-89) In total, more than 200 different species of bird have been seen at the [RSPB](/wiki/RSPB "RSPB") reserve at [Conwy](/wiki/Conwy "Conwy"), including seasonal visitors.[[86]](#cite_note-90) Larger mammals, including brown bears, wolves and wildcats, died out during the Norman period. Today, mammals include shrews, voles, badgers, otters, stoats, weasels, hedgehogs and fifteen species of bat. Two species of small rodent, the [yellow-necked mouse](/wiki/Yellow-necked_mouse "Yellow-necked mouse") and the [dormouse](/wiki/Hazel_dormouse "Hazel dormouse"), are of special Welsh note being found at the historically undisturbed border area.[[87]](#cite_note-Davies533-91) The [pine marten](/wiki/Pine_marten "Pine marten"), which has been sighted occasionally, has been reintroduced in parts of Wales since 2015, having previously not been officially recorded since the 1950s.[[88]](#cite_note-92) The [polecat](/wiki/European_polecat "European polecat") was nearly driven to extinction in Britain, but hung on in Wales and is now rapidly spreading. [Feral goats](/wiki/Feral_goat "Feral goat") can be found in Snowdonia.[[89]](#cite_note-93) In March 2021, [Natural Resources Wales](/wiki/Natural_Resources_Wales "Natural Resources Wales") (NRW) granted a licence to release up to six [beavers](/wiki/Beaver "Beaver") in the [Dyfi Valley](/wiki/River_Dyfi "River Dyfi"), the first official beaver release in Wales.[[90]](#cite_note-94)

Believed to be home to some of Wales's rarest land invertebrates, some 2,500 disused coal tips are the subject of study by the Welsh Government; the tips are home to a wide variety of other wildlife.[[91]](#cite_note-95)

The waters of south-west Wales of Gower, Pembrokeshire and Cardigan Bay attract marine animals, including [basking sharks](/wiki/Basking_sharks "Basking sharks"), Atlantic [grey seals](/wiki/Grey_seal "Grey seal"), leatherback turtles, dolphins, [porpoises](/wiki/Porpoises "Porpoises"), jellyfish, crabs and lobsters. Pembrokeshire and Ceredigion, in particular, are recognised as an area of international importance for [bottlenose dolphins](/wiki/Bottlenose_dolphin "Bottlenose dolphin"), and [New Quay](/wiki/New_Quay "New Quay") has the only summer residence of bottlenose dolphins in the whole of the UK. Freshwater fish of note include [char](/wiki/Salvelinus "Salvelinus"), eel, [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon"), [shad](/wiki/Shad "Shad"), [sparling](/wiki/European_smelt "European smelt") and [Arctic char](/wiki/Arctic_char "Arctic char"), while the [gwyniad](/wiki/Gwyniad "Gwyniad") is unique to Wales, found only in [Llyn Tegid](/wiki/Llyn_Tegid "Llyn Tegid") (Bala Lake). Wales is known for its shellfish, including [cockles](/wiki/Cockle_(bivalve) "Cockle (bivalve)"), [limpet](/wiki/Limpet "Limpet"), [mussels](/wiki/Mussel "Mussel") and [periwinkles](/wiki/Common_periwinkle "Common periwinkle"). [Herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring"), [mackerel](/wiki/Mackerel "Mackerel") and [hake](/wiki/Hake "Hake") are the more common of the country's marine fish.[[92]](#cite_note-Hist_286-288-96) The north facing high grounds of Snowdonia support a [relict](/wiki/Relict_(biology) "Relict (biology)") pre-glacial flora including the iconic Snowdon lily – *[Gagea serotina](/wiki/Gagea_serotina "Gagea serotina")* – and other [alpine](/wiki/Alpine_climate "Alpine climate") species such as *[Saxifraga cespitosa](/wiki/Saxifraga_cespitosa "Saxifraga cespitosa")*, *[Saxifraga oppositifolia](/wiki/Saxifraga_oppositifolia "Saxifraga oppositifolia")* and *[Silene acaulis](/wiki/Silene_acaulis "Silene acaulis")*. Wales has a number of plant species not found elsewhere in the UK, including the spotted rock-rose *[Tuberaria guttata](/wiki/Tuberaria_guttata "Tuberaria guttata")* on Anglesey and *[Draba aizoides](/wiki/Draba_aizoides "Draba aizoides")* on the Gower.[[93]](#cite_note-97)

## Government and politics

Main article: [Politics of Wales](/wiki/Politics_of_Wales "Politics of Wales")

See also: [Politics of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Politics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Politics of the United Kingdom") and [Welsh devolution](/wiki/Welsh_devolution "Welsh devolution")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Senedd.JPG/250px-Senedd.JPG)](/wiki/File:Senedd.JPG)

The [Senedd building](/wiki/Senedd_building "Senedd building"), designed by [Richard Rogers](/wiki/Richard_Rogers "Richard Rogers"), opened on [St David's Day](/wiki/St_David%27s_Day "St David's Day") 2006.

Wales is a country that is part of the sovereign state of the United Kingdom.[[14]](#cite_note-ONS_Geography_Guide-18) [ISO 3166-2:GB](/wiki/ISO_3166-2:GB "ISO 3166-2:GB") formerly defined Wales as a principality, with England and Scotland defined as countries and Northern Ireland as a province.[[94]](#cite_note-ISO_3166-2_2011_newsletter-98) However, this definition was raised in the Welsh Assembly in 2010 and the then [Counsel General for Wales](/wiki/Counsel_General_for_Wales "Counsel General for Wales"), [John Griffiths](/wiki/John_Griffiths_(Welsh_politician) "John Griffiths (Welsh politician)"), stated, 'Principality is a misnomer and that Wales should properly be referred to as a country.'[[95]](#cite_note-99) In 2011, ISO 3166-2:GB was updated and the term 'principality' was replaced with 'country'.[[94]](#cite_note-ISO_3166-2_2011_newsletter-98) UK Government toponymic guidelines state that, 'though there is a Prince of Wales, this role is deemed to be titular rather than exerting executive authority, and therefore Wales is described as a country rather than a principality.'[[96]](#cite_note-100)

In the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") – the 650-member [lower house](/wiki/Lower_house "Lower house") of the UK Parliament – there are 32 [members of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)") (MPs) who [represent Welsh constituencies](/wiki/List_of_MPs_for_constituencies_in_Wales_(2024%E2%80%93present) "List of MPs for constituencies in Wales (2024–present)"). At the [2024 general election](/wiki/2024_United_Kingdom_general_election_in_Wales "2024 United Kingdom general election in Wales"), 27 [Labour](/wiki/Welsh_Labour "Welsh Labour") and [Labour Co-op](/wiki/Labour_Co-op "Labour Co-op") MPs were elected, along with 4 [Plaid Cymru](/wiki/Plaid_Cymru "Plaid Cymru") MPs and 1 [Liberal Democrat](/wiki/Welsh_Liberal_Democrats "Welsh Liberal Democrats") MP from Wales.[[97]](#cite_note-Jones-2017-101)[[98]](#cite_note-102) The [Wales Office](/wiki/Wales_Office "Wales Office") is a department of the UK government responsible for Wales, whose minister, the [Secretary of State for Wales](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Wales "Secretary of State for Wales") (Welsh secretary), sits in the [UK cabinet](/wiki/UK_cabinet "UK cabinet").[[99]](#cite_note-103)

Wales has a [devolved](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom"), [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameral "Unicameral") legislature known as the [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd") (Senedd Cymru – Welsh Parliament) which holds devolved powers from the UK Parliament via a reserved powers model.[[100]](#cite_note-104)

For the purposes of [local government](/wiki/Local_government_in_Wales "Local government in Wales"), Wales has been divided into 22 council areas since 1996. These "principal areas"[[101]](#cite_note-105) are responsible for the provision of all local government services.[[102]](#cite_note-106)

### Devolved government

Main articles: [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government") and [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/First_Minister_meeting_with_First_Minister_of_Wales_-_52943408033.jpg/250px-First_Minister_meeting_with_First_Minister_of_Wales_-_52943408033.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_Minister_meeting_with_First_Minister_of_Wales_-_52943408033.jpg)

First Minister [Mark Drakeford](/wiki/Mark_Drakeford "Mark Drakeford") meets with First Minister of Scotland [Humza Yousaf](/wiki/Humza_Yousaf "Humza Yousaf") in Edinburgh, 2023

Following [devolution](/wiki/Devolution_in_the_United_Kingdom "Devolution in the United Kingdom") in 1997, the [Government of Wales Act 1998](/wiki/Government_of_Wales_Act_1998 "Government of Wales Act 1998") created a Welsh devolved assembly, the [National Assembly for Wales](/wiki/National_Assembly_for_Wales "National Assembly for Wales"), with the power to determine how Wales's central government budget is spent and administered.[[103]](#cite_note-Wyn_Jones-107) Eight years later, the [Government of Wales Act 2006](/wiki/Government_of_Wales_Act_2006 "Government of Wales Act 2006") reformed the [National Assembly for Wales](/wiki/National_Assembly_for_Wales "National Assembly for Wales") and allowed further powers to be granted to it more easily. The Act also created a system of government with a separate executive, the Welsh Government, drawn from and accountable to the legislature, the National Assembly. Following a successful referendum in 2011, the National Assembly was empowered to make laws, known as Acts of the Assembly, on all matters in devolved subject areas, without requiring the UK Parliament's approval of legislative competence. It also gained powers to raise taxes.[[104]](#cite_note-Expert_Panel-108): 33–34 In May 2020, the National Assembly was renamed "Senedd Cymru" or "the Welsh Parliament", commonly known as the Senedd in both English and Welsh.[[105]](#cite_note-Senedd_Cymru_name-109)

Devolved areas of responsibility include agriculture, economic development, education, health, housing, local government, social services, tourism, transport and the Welsh language.[[106]](#cite_note-110) The Welsh Government also promotes Welsh interests abroad.[[107]](#cite_note-111)

### Law

Main articles: [Welsh law](/wiki/Welsh_law "Welsh law"), [Law of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Law_of_the_United_Kingdom "Law of the United Kingdom"), and [English law](/wiki/English_law "English law")

[![A half timbered building of two floors, with four sets of leaded windows to the front aspect and one set to the side. The build has a steep, slate roof, with a single chimney placed left of centre. Steps and a ramp lead up to its single visible entrance](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/The_Old_Court_House_Ruthin_Wales.jpg/250px-The_Old_Court_House_Ruthin_Wales.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Old_Court_House_Ruthin_Wales.jpg)

The Old Court House, [Ruthin](/wiki/Ruthin "Ruthin"), Denbighshire, built 1401, following [Owain Glyndŵr](/wiki/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r "Owain Glyndŵr")'s attack on the town

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/c/cf/Laws_of_Hywel_Dda_%28f.4.r%29_Judge_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Laws_of_Hywel_Dda_(f.4.r)_Judge_cropped.jpg)

Illustration of a Welsh judge from the *[Laws of Hywel Dda](/wiki/Laws_of_Hywel_Dda "Laws of Hywel Dda")*

By tradition, Welsh Law was compiled during an assembly held at [Whitland](/wiki/Whitland "Whitland") around 930 by [Hywel Dda](/wiki/Hywel_Dda "Hywel Dda") (*Hywel the Good*), king of most of Wales between 942 and his death in 950. *[Cyfraith Hywel](/wiki/Cyfraith_Hywel "Cyfraith Hywel")* ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") for 'law of Hywel Dda') codified the previously existing [folk laws and legal customs](/wiki/Celtic_Law "Celtic Law") that had evolved in Wales over centuries. Welsh Law emphasised the payment of compensation for a crime to the victim, or the victim's kin, rather than punishment by the ruler.[[108]](#cite_note-HMCS-112)[[109]](#cite_note-113)[[110]](#cite_note-114) Other than in the [Marches](/wiki/Welsh_Marches "Welsh Marches"), where [March law](/wiki/March_law "March law") was imposed by the Marcher Lords, Welsh Law remained in force only for civil cases after the [Statute of Rhuddlan](/wiki/Statute_of_Rhuddlan "Statute of Rhuddlan") in 1284. [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England") annexed the [Principality of Wales](/wiki/Principality_of_Wales "Principality of Wales") following the death of [Llywelyn ap Gruffudd](/wiki/Llywelyn_ap_Gruffudd "Llywelyn ap Gruffudd"), and Welsh Law was replaced for criminal cases under the Statute. Marcher Law and Welsh Law remained in force until [Henry VIII of England](/wiki/Henry_VIII_of_England "Henry VIII of England") annexed the whole of Wales under the [Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542](/wiki/Laws_in_Wales_Acts_1535_and_1542 "Laws in Wales Acts 1535 and 1542") (often referred to as the Acts of Union of 1536 and 1543), after which English law applied to the whole of Wales.[[108]](#cite_note-HMCS-112)[[111]](#cite_note-115) The [Wales and Berwick Act 1746](/wiki/Wales_and_Berwick_Act_1746 "Wales and Berwick Act 1746") provided that all laws that applied to England would automatically apply to Wales (and the Anglo-Scottish border town of [Berwick](/wiki/Berwick-upon-Tweed "Berwick-upon-Tweed")) unless the law explicitly stated otherwise; this Act was repealed with regard to Wales by the [Welsh Language Act 1967](/wiki/Welsh_Language_Act_1967 "Welsh Language Act 1967"). English law has been the legal system of [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales") since 1536.[[112]](#cite_note-Wales_Hist_263-116)

English law is regarded as a [common law](/wiki/Common_law "Common law") system, with no major [codification](/wiki/Codification_(law) "Codification (law)") of the law and legal [precedents](/wiki/Precedent "Precedent") are binding as opposed to persuasive. The court system is headed by the [Supreme Court of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_Kingdom "Supreme Court of the United Kingdom") which is the highest court of appeal in the land for criminal and civil cases. The [Senior Courts of England and Wales](/wiki/Senior_Courts_of_England_and_Wales "Senior Courts of England and Wales") is the highest [court of first instance](/wiki/Court_of_first_instance "Court of first instance") as well as an [appellate court](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court"). The three divisions are the [Court of Appeal](/wiki/Court_of_Appeal_(England_and_Wales) "Court of Appeal (England and Wales)"), the [High Court of Justice](/wiki/High_Court_of_Justice "High Court of Justice"), and the [Crown Court](/wiki/Crown_Court "Crown Court"). Minor cases are heard by [magistrates' courts](/wiki/Magistrates%27_court_(England_and_Wales) "Magistrates' court (England and Wales)") or the [County Court](/wiki/County_Court_(England_and_Wales) "County Court (England and Wales)"). In 2007 the Wales and Cheshire Region (known as the [Wales and Cheshire Circuit](/wiki/Circuits_of_England_and_Wales "Circuits of England and Wales") before 2005) came to an end when Cheshire was attached to the North-Western England Region. From that point, Wales became a legal unit in its own right, although it remains part of the single [jurisdiction](/wiki/Jurisdiction "Jurisdiction") of [England and Wales](/wiki/England_and_Wales "England and Wales").[[113]](#cite_note-117)

The [Senedd](/wiki/Senedd "Senedd") has the authority to draft and approve laws outside of the [UK Parliamentary](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") system to meet the specific needs of Wales. Under powers approved by a [referendum](/wiki/2011_Welsh_devolution_referendum "2011 Welsh devolution referendum") held in March 2011, it is empowered to pass primary legislation, at the time referred to as an act of the National Assembly for Wales but now known as an [act of Senedd Cymru](/wiki/Act_of_Senedd_Cymru "Act of Senedd Cymru") in relation to twenty subjects listed in the [Government of Wales Act 2006](/wiki/Government_of_Wales_Act_2006 "Government of Wales Act 2006") such as health and education. Through this primary legislation, the [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government") can then also enact more specific [subordinate legislation](/wiki/Subordinate_legislation "Subordinate legislation").[[114]](#cite_note-118) There have however been some actions by the UK Government which has undermined devolved powers of the Welsh Government such as with the [United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020](/wiki/United_Kingdom_Internal_Market_Act_2020 "United Kingdom Internal Market Act 2020") and Pride in Place scheme 2025.[[115]](#cite_note-119)[[116]](#cite_note-120)

Wales is served by four regional police forces: [Dyfed-Powys Police](/wiki/Dyfed-Powys_Police "Dyfed-Powys Police"), [Gwent Police](/wiki/Gwent_Police "Gwent Police"), [North Wales Police](/wiki/North_Wales_Police "North Wales Police"), and [South Wales Police](/wiki/South_Wales_Police "South Wales Police").[[117]](#cite_note-121) There are five [prisons in Wales](/wiki/Prisons_in_Wales "Prisons in Wales"): four in the southern half of the country, and [one](/wiki/HM_Prison_Berwyn "HM Prison Berwyn") in [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_Industrial_Estate "Wrexham Industrial Estate"). Wales has no women's prisons: female inmates are imprisoned in England.[[118]](#cite_note-122)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of Wales](/wiki/Economy_of_Wales "Economy of Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Profile_of_Wales.png/250px-Profile_of_Wales.png)](/wiki/File:Profile_of_Wales.png)

A profile of the economy of Wales in 2012



A 2021 introduction to some of the largest companies based in Wales, including: Airbus, bipsync, HCI Pharmaceutical, ReNeuron, Deloitte, Coaltown Coffee, DMM International and Freudenberg

Over the last 250 years, Wales has been transformed from an [agricultural country](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Wales "Agriculture in Wales") to an industrial, and then to a [post-industrial economy](/wiki/Post-industrial_economy "Post-industrial economy").[[119]](#cite_note-123) In the 1950s, Wales's GDP was twice as big as Ireland's; by the 2020s, Ireland's economy was four times that of Wales. Since the Second World War, the [service sector](/wiki/Service_sector "Service sector") has come to account for the majority of jobs, a feature typifying most advanced economies.[[120]](#cite_note-124) in 2018, according to OECD and Eurostat data, gross domestic product (GDP) in Wales was £75 billion, an increase of 3.3 per cent from 2017. GDP per head in Wales in 2018 was £23,866, an increase of 2.9 per cent on 2017. This compares to Italy's GDP/capita of £25,000, Spain £22,000, Slovenia £20,000 and New Zealand £30,000.[[121]](#cite_note-125)[[122]](#cite_note-Nation_Cymru-126) In the three months to December 2017, 72.7 per cent of working-age adults [were employed](/wiki/Employment_rate "Employment rate"), compared to 75.2 per cent across the UK as a whole.[[123]](#cite_note-Wales-2018-127) For the 2018–19 fiscal year, the [Welsh fiscal deficit](/wiki/Welsh_fiscal_deficit "Welsh fiscal deficit") accounts for 19.4 per cent of Wales's estimated GDP.[[124]](#cite_note-Cardiff-128)

In 2019, Wales was a net exporter of electricity. It produced 27.9 TWh of electricity while only consuming 14.7 TWh.[[125]](#cite_note-129) In 2021, the Welsh government said that more than half the country's energy needs were being met by renewable sources, 2 per cent of which was from 363 [hydropower](/wiki/Hydroelectric_power "Hydroelectric power") projects.[[126]](#cite_note-BBC210302-130)

By UK law, Wales contributes to items that do not directly benefit Wales e.g. over £5 billion for [HS2](/wiki/HS2 "HS2") "which will damage the Welsh economy by £200m pa", according to the UK and Welsh Government's transport adviser Mark Barry. Wales also pays more in military costs than most similar-sized countries e.g. Wales pays twice the amount Ireland spends on the military.[[127]](#cite_note-131) The UK government spends £1.75bn per year on the military in Wales, which is almost as much as Wales spends on education every year (£1.8 billion in 2018/19) and five times as much as the total amount spent on the police in Wales (£365 million).[[128]](#cite_note-132)

From the middle of the 19th century until the post-war era, the mining and export of coal was the dominant industry. At its peak of production in 1913, nearly 233,000 men and women were employed in the [South Wales coalfield](/wiki/South_Wales_coalfield "South Wales coalfield"), mining 56 million tons of coal.[[129]](#cite_note-133) Cardiff was once the largest coal-exporting port in the world and, for a few years before the First World War, handled a greater tonnage of cargo than either London or Liverpool.[[130]](#cite_note-134) In the 1920s, over 40 per cent of the male Welsh population worked in [heavy industry](/wiki/Heavy_industry "Heavy industry").[[131]](#cite_note-IWA_2003-135) According to [Phil Williams](/wiki/Phil_Williams_(Welsh_politician) "Phil Williams (Welsh politician)"), the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") "devastated Wales", north and south, because of its "overwhelming dependence on coal and steel".[[131]](#cite_note-IWA_2003-135) From the mid-1970s, the Welsh economy faced massive restructuring with large numbers of jobs in heavy industry disappearing and being replaced eventually by new ones in [light industry](/wiki/Light_industry "Light industry") and in services. In the late 1970s and early 1980s, Wales was successful in attracting an above average share of [foreign direct investment](/wiki/Foreign_direct_investment "Foreign direct investment") in the UK.[[132]](#cite_note-136) Much of the new industry was essentially of a "branch (or "screwdriver") factory" type where a manufacturing plant or call centre is in Wales but the most highly-paid jobs in the company are elsewhere.[[133]](#cite_note-137)[[134]](#cite_note-Economi-138)

Poor-quality soil in much of Wales is unsuitable for crop-growing, so livestock farming has been the focus of farming. About 78 per cent of the land surface is used for agriculture.[[135]](#cite_note-139) The Welsh landscape, with its three national parks and [Blue Flag beaches](/wiki/Blue_Flag_beach "Blue Flag beach"), attracts [large numbers of tourists](/wiki/Tourism_in_Wales "Tourism in Wales"), who bolster the economy of rural areas.[[136]](#cite_note-140) Wales, like Northern Ireland, has relatively few high [value-added](/wiki/Value-added "Value-added") employment in sectors such as finance and research and development, attributable in part to a comparative lack of "economic mass" (i.e. population) – Wales lacks a large metropolitan centre.[[134]](#cite_note-Economi-138) The lack of high value-added employment is reflected in lower economic output per head relative to other regions of the UK: in 2002 it stood at 90 per cent of the EU25 average and around 80 per cent of the UK average.[[134]](#cite_note-Economi-138) In June 2008, Wales made history by becoming the first nation to be awarded [Fairtrade status](/wiki/Fairtrade_status "Fairtrade status").[[137]](#cite_note-141)

The [pound sterling](/wiki/Pound_sterling "Pound sterling") is the currency used in Wales. Numerous Welsh banks issued their own banknotes in the 19th century: the last bank to do so closed in 1908. Since then the [Bank of England](/wiki/Bank_of_England "Bank of England") has had a monopoly on the issue of banknotes in Wales.[[138]](#cite_note-142) The [Commercial Bank of Wales](/wiki/Commercial_Bank_of_Wales "Commercial Bank of Wales"), established in Cardiff by [Sir Julian Hodge](/wiki/Julian_Hodge "Julian Hodge") in 1971, was taken over by the [Bank of Scotland](/wiki/Bank_of_Scotland "Bank of Scotland") in 1988 and absorbed into its parent company in 2002.[[139]](#cite_note-143) The [Royal Mint](/wiki/Royal_Mint "Royal Mint"), which issues the [coinage](/wiki/Coins_of_the_pound_sterling "Coins of the pound sterling") circulating through the whole of the UK, has been based at a single site in [Llantrisant](/wiki/Llantrisant "Llantrisant") since 1980.[[140]](#cite_note-144) Since [decimalisation](/wiki/Decimalisation "Decimalisation"), in 1971, at least one of the coins in circulation emphasises Wales such as the 1995 and 2000 one pound coin. As at 2012, the last designs devoted to Wales saw production in 2008.[[141]](#cite_note-145)

### Transport

Main article: [Transport in Wales](/wiki/Transport_in_Wales "Transport in Wales")

#### Main roads

* The [M4 motorway](/wiki/M4_motorway "M4 motorway") running from West London to South Wales links [Newport](/wiki/Newport,_Wales "Newport, Wales"), [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff "Cardiff") and [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea"). Responsibility for the section of the motorway within Wales, from the [Second Severn Crossing](/wiki/Second_Severn_Crossing "Second Severn Crossing") to [Pont Abraham](/wiki/Pont_Abraham "Pont Abraham") services, sits with the Welsh Government.[[142]](#cite_note-146)
* The [A55 expressway](/wiki/A55_expressway "A55 expressway") has a similar role along the North Wales coast, connecting [Holyhead](/wiki/Holyhead "Holyhead") and [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_Gwynedd "Bangor, Gwynedd") with Wrexham and Flintshire. It also links to northwest England, principally [Chester](/wiki/Chester "Chester").[[143]](#cite_note-147)
* The main north-south Wales link is the [A470](/wiki/A470 "A470"), which runs from Cardiff to [Llandudno](/wiki/Llandudno "Llandudno").[[144]](#cite_note-148)

#### Rail

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Rail_network_of_Wales_2021.svg/250px-Rail_network_of_Wales_2021.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Rail_network_of_Wales_2021.svg)

Rail network of Wales, 2021

[Rail transport in Wales](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Wales "Rail transport in Wales") includes the [Wales & Borders franchise](/wiki/Wales_%26_Borders_franchise "Wales & Borders franchise"), which is overseen by the Welsh Government with most passenger services operated by [Transport for Wales Rail](/wiki/Transport_for_Wales_Rail "Transport for Wales Rail").[[145]](#cite_note-149) The Cardiff region has its own [urban rail network](/wiki/Valley_Lines "Valley Lines"). [Beeching cuts](/wiki/Beeching_cuts "Beeching cuts") in the 1960s mean that most of the remaining network is geared toward east–west travel connecting with the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") ports for ferries to Ireland.[[146]](#cite_note-150) Services between north and south Wales operate through the English cities of [Chester](/wiki/Chester "Chester") and [Hereford](/wiki/Hereford "Hereford") and towns of [Shrewsbury](/wiki/Shrewsbury "Shrewsbury"), [Gobowen for Oswestry](/wiki/Gobowen_railway_station "Gobowen railway station") and along the [Welsh Marches Line](/wiki/Welsh_Marches_Line "Welsh Marches Line"), with trains on the [Heart of Wales Line](/wiki/Heart_of_Wales_Line "Heart of Wales Line") from [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea_railway_station "Swansea railway station") to [Llandovery](/wiki/Llandovery_railway_station "Llandovery railway station"), [Llandrindod](/wiki/Llandrindod_Wells_railway_station "Llandrindod Wells railway station") and [Knighton](/wiki/Knighton_railway_station "Knighton railway station"), connecting with the [Welsh Marches line](/wiki/Welsh_Marches_line "Welsh Marches line") at [Craven Arms](/wiki/Craven_Arms_railway_station "Craven Arms railway station"). Trains in Wales are mainly diesel-powered but the [South Wales Main Line](/wiki/South_Wales_Main_Line "South Wales Main Line") branch of the [Great Western Main Line](/wiki/Great_Western_Main_Line "Great Western Main Line") used by services from [London Paddington](/wiki/London_Paddington "London Paddington") to Cardiff has [undergone electrification](/wiki/21st-century_modernisation_of_the_Great_Western_main_line "21st-century modernisation of the Great Western main line"), although the programme experienced significant delays and cost overruns.[[147]](#cite_note-151) Many of the Valley Lines are also undergoing electrification, under the [South Wales Metro](/wiki/South_Wales_Metro "South Wales Metro") project.[[148]](#cite_note-152) A [North-South railway](/wiki/Proposed_Wales_North-South_railway "Proposed Wales North-South railway") has been suggested to better link North and South Wales.[[149]](#cite_note-153)[[150]](#cite_note-Nation.Cymru-2022-154)[[151]](#cite_note-155)

#### Air and ferries

[Cardiff Airport](/wiki/Cardiff_Airport "Cardiff Airport") is the international airport of Wales. Providing links to European, African and Asian destinations,[[152]](#cite_note-156) it is about 12 miles (19 km) southwest of [Cardiff city centre](/wiki/Cardiff_city_centre "Cardiff city centre"), in the Vale of Glamorgan. Intra-Wales flights used to run between Anglesey (Valley) and Cardiff, and were operated since 2017 by [Eastern Airways](/wiki/Eastern_Airways "Eastern Airways");[[153]](#cite_note-157) as of 2022, those flights are no longer available. Other internal flights operate to northern England, Scotland and Northern Ireland.[[154]](#cite_note-158) Wales has four commercial ferry ports. Regular ferry services to Ireland operate from [Holyhead](/wiki/Holyhead "Holyhead"), [Pembroke Dock](/wiki/Pembroke_Dock "Pembroke Dock") and [Fishguard](/wiki/Fishguard "Fishguard"). The Swansea to [Cork](/wiki/Cork_(city) "Cork (city)") service was cancelled in 2006, reinstated in March 2010, and withdrawn again in 2012.[[155]](#cite_note-159)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Wales](/wiki/Demographics_of_Wales "Demographics of Wales")

### Population history

The population of Wales doubled from 587,000 in 1801 to 1,163,000 in 1851 and had reached 2,421,000 by 1911. Most of the increase came in the coal mining districts, especially [Glamorganshire](/wiki/Glamorganshire "Glamorganshire"), which grew from 71,000 in 1801 to 232,000 in 1851 and 1,122,000 in 1911.[[159]](#cite_note-163) Part of this increase can be attributed to the [demographic transition](/wiki/Demographic_transition "Demographic transition") seen in most industrialising countries during the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution_in_Wales "Industrial Revolution in Wales"), as death rates dropped and birth rates remained steady. However, there was also large-scale migration into Wales during the Industrial Revolution. The English were the most numerous group, but there were also considerable numbers of Irish and smaller numbers of other ethnic groups,[[160]](#cite_note-164) including [Italians](/wiki/Welsh_Italians "Welsh Italians"), who migrated to South Wales.[[161]](#cite_note-165) Wales also received immigration from various parts of the British [Commonwealth of Nations](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") in the 20th century, and [African-Caribbean](/wiki/Black_British_people "Black British people") and [Asian](/wiki/British_Asian "British Asian") communities add to the ethnocultural mix, particularly in urban Wales. Many of these self-identify as Welsh.[[162]](#cite_note-166)

The population in 1972 stood at 2.74 million and remained broadly static for the rest of the decade. However, in the early 1980s, the population fell due to net [migration](/wiki/Human_migration "Human migration") out of Wales. Since the 1980s, net migration has generally been inward, and has contributed more to [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth") than [natural change](/wiki/Sub-replacement_fertility "Sub-replacement fertility").[[163]](#cite_note-Overview-167) The resident population of Wales in 2021 according to the [census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census") was 3,107,500 (1,586,600 female and 1,521,000 male), an increase of 1.4 per cent over 2011. A decreased change from the 5 per cent increase between 2001 and 2011.[[164]](#cite_note-Region_populations-168) Wales accounted for 5.2 per cent of the [population of England and Wales](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_United_Kingdom "Demographics of the United Kingdom") in 2021. [Wales has seven cities](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Wales "List of cities in Wales"): Cardiff, Newport, Swansea, Wrexham, [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor,_Gwynedd "Bangor, Gwynedd"), [St Asaph](/wiki/St_Asaph "St Asaph") and [St Davids](/wiki/St_Davids "St Davids"). (The last two of these have [city status in the United Kingdom](/wiki/City_status_in_the_United_Kingdom "City status in the United Kingdom") despite their small populations.)[[165]](#cite_note-169) Wrexham, north Wales's largest settlement, became Wales's newest and seventh city in September [2022](/wiki/Platinum_Jubilee_Civic_Honours "Platinum Jubilee Civic Honours").[[166]](#cite_note-170)
