### Ethnicity

Main article: [Demographics of Wales § Ethnicity](/wiki/Demographics_of_Wales#Ethnicity "Demographics of Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Shirley_Bassey_%281971%29.jpg/250px-Shirley_Bassey_%281971%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shirley_Bassey_(1971).jpg)

Singer [Shirley Bassey](/wiki/Shirley_Bassey "Shirley Bassey")

The 2021 census showed that 93.8 per cent of the population of Wales identified as "White", compared to 95.6 per cent in 2011. 90.6 per cent of the population identified as "White: Welsh, English, Scottish, Northern Irish or British" in 2021. The second-highest ethnicity in 2021 was "Asian, Asian Welsh or Asian British" at 2.9 per cent of the population, compared to 2.3 per cent in 2011. 1.6 per cent of the population identified as "Mixed or multiple ethnic groups", compared to 1.0 per cent in 2011; 0.9 per cent of the population identified as "Black, Black Welsh, Black British, Caribbean or African", compared to 0.6 per cent in 2011; and 0.9 per cent identified as "Other ethnic group" compared to 0.5 per cent in 2011. The local authorities with the highest proportions of "high-level" ethnic groups other than "White" were mainly urban areas including Cardiff, Newport and Swansea. 5.3 per cent of households in Wales were multiple ethnic group households, up from 4.2 per cent in 2011.[[167]](#cite_note-Wales-2022-171)

In 2021, the first statue of a named, non-fictional woman outdoors was raised for Wales's first black headteacher, [Betty Campbell](/wiki/Betty_Campbell "Betty Campbell"). In 2023, [Patti Flynn](/wiki/Patti_Flynn "Patti Flynn") (a contemporary of [Shirley Bassey](/wiki/Shirley_Bassey "Shirley Bassey"), both of [Tiger Bay](/wiki/Tiger_Bay "Tiger Bay"), Cardiff) became the first black Welsh woman to be awarded a purple plaque.[[168]](#cite_note-172)

In 2024, [Vaughan Gething](/wiki/Vaughan_Gething "Vaughan Gething") was elected First Minister of Wales becoming the first black head of government in Europe; he had previously served as Secretary for Finance.[[169]](#cite_note-173)

### National identity

Main article: [Welsh national identity](/wiki/Welsh_national_identity "Welsh national identity")

The 2021 census showed that 55.2 per cent identified as "Welsh only" and 8.1 per cent identified as "Welsh and British", giving the combined proportion of 63.3 per cent for people identifying as Welsh.[[167]](#cite_note-Wales-2022-171) The Welsh Annual Population Survey showed that the proportion of people who identified as Welsh versus another identity was 62.3 per cent in 2022, compared to 69.2 per cent in 2001.[[170]](#cite_note-174) A 2022 YouGov poll found that 21 per cent considered themselves Welsh not British, 15 per cent more Welsh than British, 24 per cent equally Welsh and British, 7 per cent more British than Welsh, 20 per cent British and not Welsh, and 8 per cent other; a total of 67 per cent thus considered themselves Welsh to some degree.[[171]](#cite_note-YouGov-175)

### Language

Main article: [Languages of Wales](/wiki/Languages_of_Wales "Languages of Wales")

See also: [Welsh language](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") and [Welsh-speaking population](/wiki/Welsh-speaking_population "Welsh-speaking population")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png/250px-Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png)](/wiki/File:Welsh_speakers_in_the_2011_census.png)

The proportion of respondents in the 2011 census who said they could speak Welsh

Welsh is an official language in Wales as legislated by the Welsh Language (Wales) Measure 2011.[[172]](#cite_note-176) Both Welsh and English are also official languages of the Senedd.[[173]](#cite_note-177) The proportion of the Welsh population able to speak the Welsh language fell from just under 50 per cent in 1901 to 43.5 per cent in 1911, and continued to fall to 18.9 per cent in 1981.[[174]](#cite_note-ONS-2021-178) The results of the 2001 Census showed an increase in the number of Welsh speakers to 21 per cent of the population aged 3 and older, compared with 18.7 per cent in 1991 and 19 per cent in 1981. This compares with a pattern of steady decline indicated by census results during the 20th century.[[174]](#cite_note-ONS-2021-178) In the 2011 census it was recorded that the proportion of people able to speak Welsh had dropped from 20.8 per cent to 19 per cent (still higher than 1991). Despite an increase in the overall size of the Welsh population this still meant that the number of Welsh speakers in Wales dropped from 582,000 in 2001 to 562,000 in 2011. However, this figure was still higher than the 508,000 people (or 18.7 per cent of the population) who said they could speak Welsh in the 1991 census.[[175]](#cite_note-179)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Welsh_spelling_on_road_sign%2C_Newtown%2C_Powys_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1319438.jpg/250px-Welsh_spelling_on_road_sign%2C_Newtown%2C_Powys_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1319438.jpg)](/wiki/File:Welsh_spelling_on_road_sign,_Newtown,_Powys_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1319438.jpg)

Bilingual [road sign](/wiki/Road_sign "Road sign") in Welsh and English

According to the [2021 census](/wiki/2021_United_Kingdom_census "2021 United Kingdom census"), the Welsh-speaking population of Wales aged three or older was 17.8 per cent (538,300 people) and nearly three-quarters of the population in Wales said they had no Welsh language skills.[[176]](#cite_note-180) Other estimates suggest that 29.7 per cent (899,500) of people aged three or older in Wales could speak Welsh in June 2022.[[177]](#cite_note-gov-wales-2021-2022-lang-survey-181)

English is spoken by almost all people in Wales and is the main language in most of the country. [Code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") is common in all parts of Wales and is known by various terms, though none is recognised by professional linguists.[[178]](#cite_note-Davies_262-182) "[Wenglish](/wiki/Wenglish "Wenglish")" is the Welsh dialect of the English language. It has been influenced significantly by Welsh grammar and includes words derived from Welsh.[[179]](#cite_note-183) Northern and western Wales retain many areas where Welsh is spoken as a first language by the majority of the population, and English learnt as a second language. Although [monoglotism](/wiki/Monolingualism "Monolingualism") in young children continues, life-long monoglotism in Welsh no longer occurs.[[180]](#cite_note-184)

Since [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") joined the European Union, Wales has seen a significant increase in Polish immigrants. This has made [Polish](/wiki/Polish_language "Polish language") the most common main language in Wales after English and Welsh, at 0.7 per cent of the population.[[181]](#cite_note-185)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Wales](/wiki/Religion_in_Wales "Religion in Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/StDavidsCathedral_Tower%26SouthTransept.JPG/250px-StDavidsCathedral_Tower%26SouthTransept.JPG)](/wiki/File:StDavidsCathedral_Tower%26SouthTransept.JPG)

[St. David's Cathedral](/wiki/St._David%27s_Cathedral "St. David's Cathedral"), Pembrokeshire

Forms of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_Wales "Christianity in Wales") have dominated religious life in what is now Wales for more than 1,400 years.[[182]](#cite_note-186)[[183]](#cite_note-187)
The 2021 census recorded that 46.5 per cent had "No religion", more than any single religious affiliation and up from 32.1 per cent in 2011.[[167]](#cite_note-Wales-2022-171) The largest religion in Wales is Christianity, with 43.6 per cent of the population describing themselves as Christian in the 2021 census.[[167]](#cite_note-Wales-2022-171) The [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint") of Wales is [Saint David](/wiki/Saint_David "Saint David") (*Dewi Sant*), with [Saint David's Day](/wiki/Saint_David%27s_Day "Saint David's Day") (*Dydd Gŵyl Dewi Sant*) celebrated annually on 1 March.[[184]](#cite_note-188) The early 20th century saw a religious revival, the [1904–1905 Welsh Revival](/wiki/1904%E2%80%931905_Welsh_Revival "1904–1905 Welsh Revival"), which started through the evangelism of [Evan Roberts](/wiki/Evan_Roberts_(minister) "Evan Roberts (minister)") and brought large numbers of converts, sometimes whole communities, to non-Anglican Christianity.[[185]](#cite_note-189)

The [Church in Wales](/wiki/Church_in_Wales "Church in Wales") with 56,000 adherents has the largest attendance of the denominations.[[186]](#cite_note-Faith_in_Wales-190) It is a province of the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), and was part of the Church of England until disestablishment in 1920 under the [Welsh Church Act 1914](/wiki/Welsh_Church_Act_1914 "Welsh Church Act 1914"). The first [Independent Church](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)") in Wales was founded at [Llanvaches](/wiki/Llanvaches "Llanvaches") in 1638 by [William Wroth](/wiki/William_Wroth "William Wroth"). The [Presbyterian Church of Wales](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Wales "Presbyterian Church of Wales") was born out of the [Welsh Methodist revival](/wiki/Welsh_Methodist_revival "Welsh Methodist revival") in the 18th century and seceded from the [Church of England](/wiki/Church_of_England "Church of England") in 1811.[[187]](#cite_note-191) The second largest attending faith in Wales is [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Roman Catholicism in the United Kingdom"), with an estimated 43,000 adherents.[[186]](#cite_note-Faith_in_Wales-190)

Non-Christian religions are small in Wales, making up approximately 2.7 per cent of the population.[[188]](#cite_note-2011_Census_Faith-192) [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_the_United_Kingdom "Islam in the United Kingdom") is the largest, with 24,000 (0.8 per cent) reported Muslims in the 2011 census.[[188]](#cite_note-2011_Census_Faith-192) There are also communities of [Hindus](/wiki/Hinduism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Hinduism in the United Kingdom") and [Sikhs](/wiki/Sikhism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Sikhism in the United Kingdom"), mainly in the south Wales cities of Newport, Cardiff and Swansea, while the largest concentration of [Buddhists](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") is in the western rural county of [Ceredigion](/wiki/Ceredigion "Ceredigion").[[189]](#cite_note-193) [Judaism](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Wales "History of the Jews in Wales") was the first non-Christian faith to be established in Wales since Roman times, though by 2001 the community had declined to approximately 2,000[[190]](#cite_note-194) and as of 2019 only numbers in the hundreds.[[191]](#cite_note-195)

### Education

Main articles: [Education in Wales](/wiki/Education_in_Wales "Education in Wales") and [History of education in Wales](/wiki/History_of_education_in_Wales "History of education in Wales")

See also: [List of universities in Wales](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Wales "List of universities in Wales"), [List of further education colleges in Wales](/wiki/List_of_further_education_colleges_in_Wales "List of further education colleges in Wales"), and [Lists of schools in Wales](/wiki/Lists_of_schools_in_Wales "Lists of schools in Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Llanbedr_Pont_Steffan.jpg/250px-Llanbedr_Pont_Steffan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Llanbedr_Pont_Steffan.jpg)

St. David's Building, Lampeter campus, [University of Wales, Trinity Saint David](/wiki/University_of_Wales,_Trinity_Saint_David "University of Wales, Trinity Saint David") (*Prifysgol Cymru, Y Drindod Dewi Sant*). Founded in 1822, it is the oldest degree-awarding institution in Wales.[[192]](#cite_note-196)

A distinct education system has developed in Wales.[[193]](#cite_note-Davies238-197) Formal education before the 18th century was the preserve of the elite. The first grammar schools were established in Welsh towns such as [Ruthin](/wiki/Ruthin "Ruthin"), Brecon and Cowbridge.[[193]](#cite_note-Davies238-197) One of the first successful schooling systems was started by [Griffith Jones](/wiki/Griffith_Jones_(Llanddowror) "Griffith Jones (Llanddowror)"), who introduced the circulating schools in the 1730s; these are believed to have taught half the country's population to read.[[194]](#cite_note-Davies239-198) In the early 19th century, English became the usual language of instruction at schools in Wales. While the country's working class was largely Welsh-speaking at the time, Welsh public opinion wished for children to learn English.[[195]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes2024280,_293–296-199)[[196]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes202437–38-200) Many schools used corporal punishment to stop children from speaking Welsh in the first half of the 19th century;[[197]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes202451–52-201) the practice declined in the second half of the century.[[198]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes202466-202)[[199]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes2024125,_134-203) The British government never prohibited the use of Welsh at schools but it treated English as the assumed language of instruction.[[200]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes202497–98,_181-204) More Welsh was gradually used at schools in Welsh-speaking areas in the mid to late 19th century[[201]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes202496–97-205)[[202]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes2024160–162-206) and teaching of the language began to receive moderate government support from the late 19th century.[[203]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEJohnes2024181-207)

The [University College of Wales](/wiki/University_College_of_Wales "University College of Wales") opened in Aberystwyth in 1872. [Cardiff](/wiki/Cardiff_University "Cardiff University") and [Bangor](/wiki/Bangor_University "Bangor University") followed, and the three colleges came together in 1893 to form the [University of Wales](/wiki/University_of_Wales "University of Wales").[[194]](#cite_note-Davies239-198) The [Welsh Intermediate Education Act 1889](/wiki/Welsh_Intermediate_Education_Act_1889 "Welsh Intermediate Education Act 1889") created 95 secondary schools. The Welsh Department for the Board of Education followed in 1907, which gave Wales its first significant educational devolution.[[194]](#cite_note-Davies239-198) A resurgence in Welsh-language schools in the latter half of the 20th century at nursery and primary level saw attitudes shift towards teaching in the medium of Welsh.[[204]](#cite_note-Davies240-208) Welsh is a compulsory subject in all of Wales's state schools for pupils aged 5–16 years old.[[205]](#cite_note-209) While there has never been an exclusively Welsh-language college, Welsh-medium higher education is delivered through the individual universities and has since 2011 been supported by the [Coleg Cymraeg Cenedlaethol](/wiki/Coleg_Cymraeg_Cenedlaethol "Coleg Cymraeg Cenedlaethol") (Welsh-language National College) as a delocalised federal institution. In 2021–2022, there were 1,470 maintained schools in Wales.[[206]](#cite_note-210) In 2021–22, the country had 471,131 pupils taught by 25,210 full-time equivalent teachers.[[207]](#cite_note-211)[[208]](#cite_note-212)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Wales](/wiki/Health_in_Wales "Health in Wales")

See also: [Healthcare in Wales](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Wales "Healthcare in Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/University_Hospital_of_Wales%2C_Heath_Park_-_Cardiff_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1736088.jpg/250px-University_Hospital_of_Wales%2C_Heath_Park_-_Cardiff_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1736088.jpg)](/wiki/File:University_Hospital_of_Wales,_Heath_Park_-_Cardiff_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1736088.jpg)

[University Hospital of Wales](/wiki/University_Hospital_of_Wales "University Hospital of Wales"), Cardiff

Public healthcare in Wales is provided by [NHS Wales](/wiki/NHS_Wales "NHS Wales") (*GIG Cymru*), through [seven local health boards and three all-Wales trusts](/wiki/List_of_NHS_Wales_trusts_and_health_boards "List of NHS Wales trusts and health boards"). It was originally formed as part of the NHS structure for England and Wales by the [National Health Service Act 1946](/wiki/National_Health_Service_Act_1946 "National Health Service Act 1946"), but with powers over the NHS in Wales coming under the Secretary of State for Wales in 1969.[[209]](#cite_note-213) Responsibility for NHS Wales passed to the Welsh Assembly under devolution in 1999, and is now the responsibility of the [Minister for Health and Social Services](/wiki/Minister_for_Health_and_Social_Services "Minister for Health and Social Services").[[210]](#cite_note-WHS_2009-214) Historically, Wales was served by smaller 'cottage' hospitals, built as voluntary institutions.[[211]](#cite_note-Davies361-215) As newer, more expensive, diagnostic techniques and treatments became available, clinical work has been concentrated in newer, larger district hospitals.[[211]](#cite_note-Davies361-215) In 2006, there were seventeen district hospitals in Wales.[[211]](#cite_note-Davies361-215) NHS Wales directly employs over 90,000 staff, making it Wales's biggest employer.[[212]](#cite_note-216) The National Survey for Wales in 2021–22 reported that 72 per cent of adults surveyed had good or very good general health, 19 per cent had fair general health and 8 had bad or very bad general health.[[213]](#cite_note-217) The survey recorded that 46 per cent of Welsh adults had a long-standing illness, such as arthritis, asthma, diabetes or heart disease.[[214]](#cite_note-218) The survey also reported that 13 per cent of the adult population were [smokers](/wiki/Smoking "Smoking"), 16 per cent admitted drinking alcohol above weekly recommended guidelines, while 56 per cent undertook the recommended 150 minutes of physical activity each week.[[215]](#cite_note-219) According to the survey, 30 per cent of adults in Wales reported to have eaten at least 5 portions of fruit or vegetables the previous day and 36 per cent reported a healthy weight.[[216]](#cite_note-220)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Wales](/wiki/Culture_of_Wales "Culture of Wales")

Wales has a distinctive culture including its own language, customs, holidays and music. There are four [UNESCO World Heritage Sites in Wales](/wiki/List_of_World_Heritage_Sites_in_Wales "List of World Heritage Sites in Wales"): [The Castles and Town Walls of King Edward I in Gwynedd](/wiki/Castles_and_Town_Walls_of_King_Edward_in_Gwynedd "Castles and Town Walls of King Edward in Gwynedd"); [Pontcysyllte Aqueduct](/wiki/Pontcysyllte_Aqueduct "Pontcysyllte Aqueduct") and Canal; the [Blaenavon Industrial Landscape](/wiki/Blaenavon_Industrial_Landscape "Blaenavon Industrial Landscape"); and [The Slate Landscape of Northwest Wales](/wiki/The_Slate_Landscape_of_Northwest_Wales "The Slate Landscape of Northwest Wales").[[217]](#cite_note-221)

### Museums and libraries

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c5/National_Library_of_Wales.jpg/250px-National_Library_of_Wales.jpg)](/wiki/File:National_Library_of_Wales.jpg)

The [National Library of Wales](/wiki/National_Library_of_Wales "National Library of Wales"), [Aberystwyth](/wiki/Aberystwyth "Aberystwyth")

[Amgueddfa Cymru – Museum Wales](/wiki/Amgueddfa_Cymru_%E2%80%93_Museum_Wales "Amgueddfa Cymru – Museum Wales") was founded by [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter") in 1907 as the National Museum of Wales. It operates at seven sites: [National Museum Cardiff](/wiki/National_Museum_Cardiff "National Museum Cardiff"), [St Fagans National History Museum](/wiki/St_Fagans_National_History_Museum "St Fagans National History Museum"), [Big Pit National Coal Museum](/wiki/Big_Pit_National_Coal_Museum "Big Pit National Coal Museum"), [National Wool Museum](/wiki/National_Wool_Museum "National Wool Museum"), [National Slate Museum](/wiki/National_Slate_Museum "National Slate Museum"), [National Roman Legion Museum](/wiki/National_Roman_Legion_Museum "National Roman Legion Museum"), and the [National Waterfront Museum](/wiki/National_Waterfront_Museum "National Waterfront Museum"). Entry to all sites is free.[[218]](#cite_note-222) The [National Library of Wales](/wiki/National_Library_of_Wales "National Library of Wales"), based in [Aberystwyth](/wiki/Aberystwyth "Aberystwyth"), houses important collections of printed works, including the [Sir John Williams Collection](/wiki/National_Library_of_Wales#Sir_John_Williams_Collection "National Library of Wales") and the [Shirburn Castle](/wiki/Shirburn_Castle "Shirburn Castle") collection,[[219]](#cite_note-Davies594-223) as well as art collections including portraits and photographs, [ephemera](/wiki/Ephemera "Ephemera") and [Ordnance Survey](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey "Ordnance Survey") maps.[[219]](#cite_note-Davies594-223)

### Mythology

Main article: [Welsh mythology](/wiki/Welsh_mythology "Welsh mythology")

Remnants of native Celtic [mythology](/wiki/Mythology "Mythology") of the pre-Christian [Britons](/wiki/Britons_(historic) "Britons (historic)") was passed down orally by the *cynfeirdd* (the early poets).[[220]](#cite_note-cynfeirdd-224) Some of their work survives in later [medieval Welsh manuscripts](/wiki/Medieval_Welsh_literature "Medieval Welsh literature"): the [Black Book of Carmarthen](/wiki/Black_Book_of_Carmarthen "Black Book of Carmarthen") and the [Book of Aneirin](/wiki/Book_of_Aneirin "Book of Aneirin") (both 13th-century); the [Book of Taliesin](/wiki/Book_of_Taliesin "Book of Taliesin") and the [White Book of Rhydderch](/wiki/White_Book_of_Rhydderch "White Book of Rhydderch") (both 14th-century); and the [Red Book of Hergest](/wiki/Red_Book_of_Hergest "Red Book of Hergest") (c. 1400).[[220]](#cite_note-cynfeirdd-224) The [prose](/wiki/Prose "Prose") stories from the White and Red Books are known as the *[Mabinogion](/wiki/Mabinogion "Mabinogion")*.[[221]](#cite_note-225) Poems such as *[Cad Goddeu](/wiki/Cad_Goddeu "Cad Goddeu")* (The Battle of the Trees) and mnemonic list-texts like the *[Welsh Triads](/wiki/Welsh_Triads "Welsh Triads")* and the *[Thirteen Treasures of the Island of Britain](/wiki/Thirteen_Treasures_of_the_Island_of_Britain "Thirteen Treasures of the Island of Britain")*, also contain mythological material.[[222]](#cite_note-226) These texts include the earliest forms of the [Arthurian legend](/wiki/Arthurian_legend "Arthurian legend") and the traditional history of post-[Roman Britain](/wiki/Roman_Britain "Roman Britain").[[220]](#cite_note-cynfeirdd-224) Other sources of Welsh [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") include the 9th-century Latin historical compilation *[Historia Britonum](/wiki/Historia_Britonum "Historia Britonum")* (the History of the Britons) and [Geoffrey of Monmouth](/wiki/Geoffrey_of_Monmouth "Geoffrey of Monmouth")'s 12th-century Latin [chronicle](/wiki/Chronicle "Chronicle") *[Historia Regum Britanniae](/wiki/Historia_Regum_Britanniae "Historia Regum Britanniae")* (the History of the Kings of Britain), and later folklore, such as *The Welsh Fairy Book* by W. Jenkyn Thomas.[[223]](#cite_note-227)

### Literature

Main articles: [Literature of Wales (Welsh language)](/wiki/Literature_of_Wales_(Welsh_language) "Literature of Wales (Welsh language)"), [List of Welsh writers](/wiki/List_of_Welsh_writers "List of Welsh writers"), and [Literature of Wales (English language)](/wiki/Literature_of_Wales_(English_language) "Literature of Wales (English language)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Black_Book_of_Carmarthen_%28f.4.r%29.jpg/250px-Black_Book_of_Carmarthen_%28f.4.r%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Black_Book_of_Carmarthen_(f.4.r).jpg)

Welsh poetry from the 13th-century [Black Book of Carmarthen](/wiki/Black_Book_of_Carmarthen "Black Book of Carmarthen").

Wales has one of the oldest unbroken literary traditions in Europe[[224]](#cite_note-Davies464-228) going back to the sixth century and including [Geoffrey of Monmouth](/wiki/Geoffrey_of_Monmouth "Geoffrey of Monmouth") and [Gerald of Wales](/wiki/Gerald_of_Wales "Gerald of Wales"), regarded as among the finest Latin authors of the Middle Ages.[[224]](#cite_note-Davies464-228) The earliest body of Welsh verse, by poets [Taliesin](/wiki/Taliesin "Taliesin") and [Aneirin](/wiki/Aneirin "Aneirin"), survive not in their original form, but in much-changed, medieval versions.[[224]](#cite_note-Davies464-228) Welsh poetry and native lore and learning survived through the era of the [Poets of the Princes](/wiki/Poets_of_the_Princes "Poets of the Princes") (c. 1100–1280) and then the [Poets of the Gentry](/wiki/Medieval_Welsh_literature#Poets_of_the_Nobility,_or_Cywyddwyr_(c._1300_–_c._1600) "Medieval Welsh literature") (c. 1350–1650). The former were professional poets who composed eulogies and elegies to their patrons while the latter favoured the [cywydd](/wiki/Cywydd "Cywydd") metre.[[225]](#cite_note-Davies688-9-229) The period produced one of Wales's greatest poets, [Dafydd ap Gwilym](/wiki/Dafydd_ap_Gwilym "Dafydd ap Gwilym").[[226]](#cite_note-230) After the Anglicisation of the gentry the tradition declined.[[225]](#cite_note-Davies688-9-229)

Despite the extinction of the professional poet, the integration of the native elite into a wider cultural world did bring other literary benefits.[[227]](#cite_note-Davies465-231) Renaissance scholars such as [William Salesbury](/wiki/William_Salesbury "William Salesbury") and [John Davies](/wiki/John_Davies_(Mallwyd) "John Davies (Mallwyd)") brought [humanist](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism "Renaissance humanism") ideals from English universities.[[227]](#cite_note-Davies465-231) In 1588 [William Morgan](/wiki/William_Morgan_(Bible_translator) "William Morgan (Bible translator)") became the first person to translate the [Bible into Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_Bible "Welsh Bible").[[227]](#cite_note-Davies465-231) From the 16th century the proliferation of the 'free-metre' verse became the most important development in Welsh poetry, but from the middle of the 17th century a host of imported accentual metres from England became very popular.[[227]](#cite_note-Davies465-231) By the 19th century the creation of a Welsh epic, fuelled by the eisteddfod, became an obsession with Welsh-language writers.[[228]](#cite_note-Davies466-232) The output of this period was prolific in quantity but unequal in quality.[[229]](#cite_note-Williams121-233) Initially excluded, religious denominations came to dominate the competitions, with bardic themes becoming scriptural and didactic.[[229]](#cite_note-Williams121-233)

Developments in 19th-century Welsh literature include [Lady Charlotte Guest](/wiki/Lady_Charlotte_Guest "Lady Charlotte Guest")'s translation into English of the Mabinogion, one of the most important medieval Welsh prose works of Celtic mythology. 1885 saw the publication of *[Rhys Lewis](/wiki/Rhys_Lewis_(novel) "Rhys Lewis (novel)")* by [Daniel Owen](/wiki/Daniel_Owen "Daniel Owen"), credited as the first novel written in the Welsh language. The 20th century saw a move from the verbose Victorian Welsh style, with works such as [Thomas Gwynn Jones](/wiki/Thomas_Gwynn_Jones "Thomas Gwynn Jones")'s *[Ymadawiad Arthur](/wiki/Ymadawiad_Arthur "Ymadawiad Arthur")*.[[228]](#cite_note-Davies466-232) The First World War had a profound effect on Welsh literature with a more pessimistic style championed by [T. H. Parry-Williams](/wiki/T._H._Parry-Williams "T. H. Parry-Williams") and [R. Williams Parry](/wiki/R._Williams_Parry "R. Williams Parry").[[228]](#cite_note-Davies466-232) The industrialisation of south Wales saw a further shift with the likes of [Rhydwen Williams](/wiki/Rhydwen_Williams "Rhydwen Williams") who used the poetry and metre of a bygone rural Wales but in the context of an industrial landscape. The inter-war period is dominated by [Saunders Lewis](/wiki/Saunders_Lewis "Saunders Lewis"), for his political and reactionary views as much as his plays, poetry and criticism.[[228]](#cite_note-Davies466-232)

The careers of some 1930s writers continued after World War Two, including those of [Gwyn Thomas](/wiki/Gwyn_Thomas_(novelist) "Gwyn Thomas (novelist)"), [Vernon Watkins](/wiki/Vernon_Watkins "Vernon Watkins"), and [Dylan Thomas](/wiki/Dylan_Thomas "Dylan Thomas"), whose most famous work *[Under Milk Wood](/wiki/Under_Milk_Wood "Under Milk Wood")* was first broadcast in 1954. Thomas was one of the most notable and popular Welsh writers of the 20th century and one of the most innovative poets of his time.[[230]](#cite_note-234) The attitude of the post-war generation of Welsh writers in English towards Wales differs from the previous generation, with greater sympathy for Welsh nationalism and the Welsh language. The change is linked to the nationalism of [Saunders Lewis](/wiki/Saunders_Lewis "Saunders Lewis") and the burning of the Bombing School on the [Llŷn Peninsula](/wiki/Ll%C5%B7n_Peninsula "Llŷn Peninsula") in 1936.[[231]](#cite_note-235) In poetry [R. S. Thomas](/wiki/R._S._Thomas "R. S. Thomas") (1913–2000) was the most important figure throughout the second half of the 20th century. He "did not learn the Welsh language until he was 30 and wrote all his poems in English".[[232]](#cite_note-236) Major writers in the second half of the 20th century include [Emyr Humphreys](/wiki/Emyr_Humphreys "Emyr Humphreys") (1919–2020), who during his long writing career published over twenty novels,[[233]](#cite_note-237) and [Raymond Williams](/wiki/Raymond_Williams "Raymond Williams") (1921–1988).[[234]](#cite_note-238)

### Visual arts

Main article: [Welsh art](/wiki/Welsh_art "Welsh art")

See also: [Architecture of Wales](/wiki/Architecture_of_Wales "Architecture of Wales")

Works of [Celtic art](/wiki/Celtic_art "Celtic art") have been found in Wales.[[235]](#cite_note-239) In the [Early Medieval](/wiki/Early_Medieval "Early Medieval") period, the [Celtic Christianity](/wiki/Celtic_Christianity "Celtic Christianity") of Wales was part of the [Insular art](/wiki/Insular_art "Insular art") of the [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles"). A number of [illuminated manuscripts](/wiki/Illuminated_manuscript "Illuminated manuscript") [from Wales](/wiki/Category:Illuminated_manuscripts_of_Welsh_origin "Category:Illuminated manuscripts of Welsh origin") survive, including the 8th-century [Hereford Gospels](/wiki/Hereford_Gospels "Hereford Gospels") and [Lichfield Gospels](/wiki/Lichfield_Gospels "Lichfield Gospels"). The 11th-century [Ricemarch Psalter](/wiki/Ricemarch_Psalter "Ricemarch Psalter") (now in [Dublin](/wiki/Dublin "Dublin")) is certainly Welsh, made in [St David's](/wiki/St_David%27s "St David's"), and shows a late Insular style with unusual Viking influence.[[236]](#cite_note-240)

Some Welsh artists of the 16th–18th centuries tended to leave the country to work, moving to London or Italy. [Richard Wilson](/wiki/Richard_Wilson_(painter) "Richard Wilson (painter)") (1714–1782) is arguably the first major British landscapist; although more notable for his Italian scenes, he painted several Welsh scenes on visits from London. By the late 18th century, the popularity of [landscape art](/wiki/Landscape_art "Landscape art") grew and clients were found in the larger Welsh towns, allowing more Welsh artists to stay in their homeland. Artists from outside Wales were also drawn to paint Welsh scenery, at first because of the [Celtic Revival](/wiki/Celtic_Revival "Celtic Revival").[[237]](#cite_note-241)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Amgueddfa_Cymru_-_Y_Bardd_-_The_Bard_-_2568.jpeg/250px-Amgueddfa_Cymru_-_Y_Bardd_-_The_Bard_-_2568.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Amgueddfa_Cymru_-_Y_Bardd_-_The_Bard_-_2568.jpeg)

*[The Bard](/wiki/The_Bard_(Jones) "The Bard (Jones)")*, 1774, by [Thomas Jones](/wiki/Thomas_Jones_(artist) "Thomas Jones (artist)") (1742–1803).

The [Industrial Schools Act 1857](/wiki/Industrial_Schools_Act_1857 "Industrial Schools Act 1857") provided for the establishment of a number of art schools throughout the United Kingdom, and the [Cardiff School of Art](/wiki/University_of_Wales_Institute,_Cardiff "University of Wales Institute, Cardiff") opened in 1865. Graduates still very often had to leave Wales to work, but [Betws-y-Coed](/wiki/Betws-y-Coed "Betws-y-Coed") became a popular centre for artists, and its artists' colony helped to form the [Royal Cambrian Academy of Art](/wiki/Royal_Cambrian_Academy_of_Art "Royal Cambrian Academy of Art") in 1881.[[238]](#cite_note-242) The sculptor Sir William [Goscombe John](/wiki/Goscombe_John "Goscombe John") made works for Welsh commissions, although he had settled in London. [Christopher Williams](/wiki/Christopher_Williams_(Welsh_artist) "Christopher Williams (Welsh artist)"), whose subjects were mostly resolutely Welsh, was also based in London. [Thomas E. Stephens](/wiki/Thomas_E._Stephens_(artist) "Thomas E. Stephens (artist)")[[239]](#cite_note-243) and [Andrew Vicari](/wiki/Andrew_Vicari "Andrew Vicari") had very successful careers as portraitists, based respectively in the United States and France.[[240]](#cite_note-244)

Welsh painters gravitated towards the art capitals of Europe. [Augustus John](/wiki/Augustus_John "Augustus John") and his sister [Gwen John](/wiki/Gwen_John "Gwen John") lived mostly in London and Paris. However, the landscapists Sir [Kyffin Williams](/wiki/Kyffin_Williams "Kyffin Williams") and [Peter Prendergast](/wiki/Peter_Prendergast_(artist) "Peter Prendergast (artist)") lived in Wales for most of their lives, while remaining in touch with the wider art world. [Ceri Richards](/wiki/Ceri_Richards "Ceri Richards") was very engaged in the Welsh art scene as a teacher in Cardiff and even after moving to London; he was a figurative painter in international styles including [Surrealism](/wiki/Surrealism "Surrealism"). Various artists have moved to Wales, including [Eric Gill](/wiki/Eric_Gill "Eric Gill"), the London-Welshman [David Jones](/wiki/David_Jones_(poet) "David Jones (poet)"), and the sculptor [Jonah Jones](/wiki/Jonah_Jones_(sculptor) "Jonah Jones (sculptor)"). [The Kardomah Gang](/wiki/The_Kardomah_Gang "The Kardomah Gang") was an intellectual circle in Swansea, centred on the poet [Dylan Thomas](/wiki/Dylan_Thomas "Dylan Thomas") and the poet and artist [Vernon Watkins](/wiki/Vernon_Watkins "Vernon Watkins"), which also included the painter [Alfred Janes](/wiki/Alfred_Janes "Alfred Janes").[[241]](#cite_note-245)

South Wales had several notable [potteries](/wiki/Pottery "Pottery"), one of the first important sites being the [Ewenny Pottery](/wiki/Ewenny#Potteries "Ewenny") in [Bridgend](/wiki/Bridgend "Bridgend"), which began producing earthenware in the 17th century.[[242]](#cite_note-Davies701-246) In the 18th and 19th centuries, with more scientific methods becoming available, more refined ceramics were produced: this was led by the [Cambrian Pottery](/wiki/Cambrian_Pottery "Cambrian Pottery") (1764–1870, also known as "Swansea pottery"), and later [Nantgarw Pottery](/wiki/Nantgarw_Pottery "Nantgarw Pottery") near Cardiff, which was in operation from 1813 to 1820 making fine [porcelain](/wiki/Porcelain "Porcelain"), and then utilitarian pottery from 1833 until 1920.[[242]](#cite_note-Davies701-246) [Portmeirion Pottery](/wiki/Portmeirion_Pottery "Portmeirion Pottery"), founded in 1960 by [Susan Williams-Ellis](/wiki/Susan_Williams-Ellis "Susan Williams-Ellis") (daughter of [Clough Williams-Ellis](/wiki/Clough_Williams-Ellis "Clough Williams-Ellis"), creator of the Italianate village of [Portmeirion](/wiki/Portmeirion "Portmeirion"), [Gwynedd](/wiki/Gwynedd "Gwynedd")) is based in [Stoke-on-Trent](/wiki/Stoke-on-Trent "Stoke-on-Trent"), England.[[243]](#cite_note-247)

### Media

Main article: [Media in Wales](/wiki/Media_in_Wales "Media in Wales")

See also: [List of newspapers in Wales](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_Wales "List of newspapers in Wales"), [Radio in Wales](/wiki/Radio_in_Wales "Radio in Wales"), and [Television in Wales](/wiki/Television_in_Wales "Television in Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/19/Filming_%22Torchwood%22_in_Cardiff_%282%29.jpg/250px-Filming_%22Torchwood%22_in_Cardiff_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Filming_%22Torchwood%22_in_Cardiff_(2).jpg)

A number of BBC productions, such as *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* and *[Torchwood](/wiki/Torchwood "Torchwood")*, have been filmed in Wales.

Wales became the UK's first [digital television](/wiki/Digital_television "Digital television") nation in 2010.[[244]](#cite_note-248) [BBC Cymru Wales](/wiki/BBC_Cymru_Wales "BBC Cymru Wales") is the national broadcaster,[[245]](#cite_note-BBC_amdanyn_nhw-249) producing both television and radio programmes in Welsh and English.[[246]](#cite_note-250) It has also produced programmes such as *[Life on Mars](/wiki/Life_on_Mars_(British_TV_series) "Life on Mars (British TV series)")*, *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")* and *[Torchwood](/wiki/Torchwood "Torchwood")* for BBC's network audience across the United Kingdom.[[245]](#cite_note-BBC_amdanyn_nhw-249)[[247]](#cite_note-251) [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)"), the UK's main commercial broadcaster, has a Welsh-orientated service branded [ITV Cymru Wales](/wiki/ITV_Cymru_Wales "ITV Cymru Wales").[[248]](#cite_note-252) [S4C](/wiki/S4C "S4C") began broadcasting in 1982. Its output was mostly in Welsh at peak hours, but shared English-language content with [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") at other times. Since the [digital switchover](/wiki/Digital_switchover "Digital switchover") the channel has broadcast exclusively in Welsh.[[249]](#cite_note-Mercator-253): 13–14 [BBC Radio Cymru](/wiki/BBC_Radio_Cymru "BBC Radio Cymru") is the BBC's Welsh-language radio service, which broadcasts throughout Wales.[[245]](#cite_note-BBC_amdanyn_nhw-249) A number of independent radio stations broadcast in the Welsh regions, predominantly in English. In 2006, several regional radio stations broadcast in Welsh: output ranged from two two-minute news bulletins each weekday ([Radio Maldwyn](/wiki/Radio_Maldwyn "Radio Maldwyn")) to over 14 hours of Welsh-language programmes weekly ([Swansea Sound](/wiki/Swansea_Sound "Swansea Sound")) to essentially bilingual stations such as [Heart Cymru](/wiki/Heart_Cymru "Heart Cymru") and [Radio Ceredigion](/wiki/Radio_Ceredigion "Radio Ceredigion").[[249]](#cite_note-Mercator-253): 16–18

Most of the newspapers sold and read in Wales are national newspapers available throughout Britain. The *[Western Mail](/wiki/Western_Mail_(Wales) "Western Mail (Wales)")* is Wales's only print national daily newspaper.[[250]](#cite_note-BBC_papurau-254) Wales-based regional daily newspapers include the *[Daily Post](/wiki/Daily_Post_(North_Wales) "Daily Post (North Wales)")* (which covers North Wales), the *[South Wales Evening Post](/wiki/South_Wales_Evening_Post "South Wales Evening Post")* (Swansea), the *[South Wales Echo](/wiki/South_Wales_Echo "South Wales Echo")* (Cardiff), and the *[South Wales Argus](/wiki/South_Wales_Argus "South Wales Argus")* (Newport).[[250]](#cite_note-BBC_papurau-254) *[Y Cymro](/wiki/Y_Cymro "Y Cymro")* is a Welsh-language newspaper, published weekly.[[249]](#cite_note-Mercator-253): 22–23 *Wales on Sunday* is the only Welsh Sunday newspaper that covers the whole of Wales.[[251]](#cite_note-255) The [Books Council of Wales](/wiki/Books_Council_of_Wales "Books Council of Wales") is the Welsh-Government-funded body tasked with promoting Welsh literature in Welsh and English.[[252]](#cite_note-256) The BCW provides publishing grants for qualifying English- and Welsh-language publications.[[253]](#cite_note-257) Around 650 books are published each year, by some of the dozens of Welsh publishers.[[249]](#cite_note-Mercator-253): 34[[254]](#cite_note-258) Wales's main publishing houses include [Gomer Press](/wiki/Gomer_Press "Gomer Press"), [Gwasg Carreg Gwalch](/wiki/Gwasg_Carreg_Gwalch "Gwasg Carreg Gwalch"), [Honno](/wiki/Honno_(press) "Honno (press)"), the [University of Wales Press](/wiki/University_of_Wales_Press "University of Wales Press") and [Y Lolfa](/wiki/Y_Lolfa "Y Lolfa").[[249]](#cite_note-Mercator-253): 34 Journals with a Welsh focus include *Cambria* (a Welsh affairs magazine published bi-monthly in English),[[255]](#cite_note-259) *Planet*, and *[Poetry Wales](/wiki/Poetry_Wales "Poetry Wales")*.[[256]](#cite_note-260) Welsh-language magazines include the current affairs titles *[Golwg](/wiki/Golwg "Golwg")* ("View"), published weekly, and *[Barn](/wiki/Barn_(Welsh_magazine) "Barn (Welsh magazine)")* ("Opinion"), published monthly.[[249]](#cite_note-Mercator-253) *Y Wawr* ("The Dawn") is published quarterly by *[Merched y Wawr](/wiki/Merched_y_Wawr "Merched y Wawr")*, the national organisation for women.[[249]](#cite_note-Mercator-253) *[Y Traethodydd](/wiki/Y_Traethodydd "Y Traethodydd")* ("The Essayist"), a quarterly publication by the [Presbyterian Church of Wales](/wiki/Presbyterian_Church_of_Wales "Presbyterian Church of Wales"), first appeared in 1845 and is the oldest Welsh publication still in print.[[249]](#cite_note-Mercator-253)

### Performing arts

#### Music and festivals

Main articles: [Music of Wales](/wiki/Music_of_Wales "Music of Wales") and [List of festivals in Wales](/wiki/List_of_festivals_in_Wales "List of festivals in Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Sir_Tom_Jones_at_The_Queen%27s_Birthday_Party_%28cropped-2%29.jpg/250px-Sir_Tom_Jones_at_The_Queen%27s_Birthday_Party_%28cropped-2%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sir_Tom_Jones_at_The_Queen%27s_Birthday_Party_(cropped-2).jpg)

Singer [Tom Jones](/wiki/Tom_Jones_(singer) "Tom Jones (singer)")

Wales, "the land of song", is notable for its solo artists, its [male voice choirs](/wiki/Men%27s_chorus "Men's chorus") and its harpists.[[257]](#cite_note-261) The annual *[National Eisteddfod](/wiki/National_Eisteddfod "National Eisteddfod")* is the country's main performance festival. The *Llangollen [International Eisteddfod](/wiki/International_Eisteddfod "International Eisteddfod")* provides an opportunity for the singers and musicians of the world to perform. The Welsh Folk Song Society publishes collections of historical songs and tunes.[[258]](#cite_note-262) Traditional instruments of Wales include the *telyn deires* ([triple harp](/wiki/Triple_harp "Triple harp")), fiddle, *[crwth](/wiki/Crwth "Crwth")* (bowed lyre) and the *pibgorn* (hornpipe).[[259]](#cite_note-263) Male voice choirs emerged in the 19th century, formed as the tenor and bass sections of chapel choirs, and embraced the popular secular hymns of the day.[[260]](#cite_note-Davies532-264) Welsh congregations and choirs were known for singing in a rousing four-voice style, becoming characteristic of the country.[[261]](#cite_note-265) Many of the historic choirs survive in modern Wales, singing a mixture of traditional and popular songs.[[260]](#cite_note-Davies532-264)

The [BBC National Orchestra of Wales](/wiki/BBC_National_Orchestra_of_Wales "BBC National Orchestra of Wales") performs in Wales and internationally. The [Welsh National Opera](/wiki/Welsh_National_Opera "Welsh National Opera") is based at the [Wales Millennium Centre](/wiki/Wales_Millennium_Centre "Wales Millennium Centre") in [Cardiff Bay](/wiki/Cardiff_Bay "Cardiff Bay"), while the [National Youth Orchestra of Wales](/wiki/National_Youth_Orchestra_of_Wales "National Youth Orchestra of Wales") was the first of its type in the world.[[262]](#cite_note-266) Wales has a tradition of producing notable singers in both the classical and pop arenas,[[263]](#cite_note-267) as well as some popular bands.[[264]](#cite_note-268)[[265]](#cite_note-269)[[266]](#cite_note-270) The Welsh [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") scene has enjoyed a resurgence in the 21st century.[[267]](#cite_note-271)

#### Drama

See also: [Cinema of Wales](/wiki/Cinema_of_Wales "Cinema of Wales") and [List of Welsh television series](/wiki/List_of_Welsh_television_series "List of Welsh television series")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Catherine_Zeta-Jones_VF_2012_Shankbone_2.jpg/250px-Catherine_Zeta-Jones_VF_2012_Shankbone_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Catherine_Zeta-Jones_VF_2012_Shankbone_2.jpg)

[Catherine Zeta-Jones](/wiki/Catherine_Zeta-Jones "Catherine Zeta-Jones"), born in [Swansea](/wiki/Swansea "Swansea")

The earliest surviving Welsh plays are two medieval [miracle plays](/wiki/Miracle_play "Miracle play"), *Y Tri Brenin o Gwlen* ("The three Kings from Cologne") and *Y Dioddefaint a'r Atgyfodiad* ("The Passion and the Resurrection").[[268]](#cite_note-272) A recognised Welsh tradition of theatre emerged during the 18th century, in the form of an [interlude](/wiki/Play_(theatre) "Play (theatre)"), a metrical play performed at fairs and markets.[[269]](#cite_note-273) Drama in the early 20th century thrived, but the country established neither a Welsh National Theatre nor a national ballet company.[[270]](#cite_note-Davies192-274) After the Second World War, the substantial number of amateur theatre companies reduced by two-thirds.[[271]](#cite_note-Davies224-275) Competition from television in the mid-20th century led to greater professionalism in the theatre.[[271]](#cite_note-Davies224-275) Plays by [Emlyn Williams](/wiki/Emlyn_Williams "Emlyn Williams") and [Alun Owen](/wiki/Alun_Owen "Alun Owen") and others were staged, while Welsh actors, including [Richard Burton](/wiki/Richard_Burton "Richard Burton") and [Anthony Hopkins](/wiki/Anthony_Hopkins "Anthony Hopkins"), were establishing international reputations.[[271]](#cite_note-Davies224-275)[[272]](#cite_note-276)[[273]](#cite_note-277) Wales has also produced some well-known comedians.[[274]](#cite_note-278)

#### Dance

See also: [Welsh dance](/wiki/Welsh_dance "Welsh dance") and [Welsh stepdance](/wiki/Welsh_stepdance "Welsh stepdance")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Traditional_Welsh_dance_and_dress%2C_Senedd%2C_St_David%27s_Day_2009_Gwisgoedd_a_dawnsio_traddodiadol_Gymreig%2C_Senedd%2C_Dydd_G%C5%B5yl_Dewi_2009_%283678719264%29.jpg/250px-Traditional_Welsh_dance_and_dress%2C_Senedd%2C_St_David%27s_Day_2009_Gwisgoedd_a_dawnsio_traddodiadol_Gymreig%2C_Senedd%2C_Dydd_G%C5%B5yl_Dewi_2009_%283678719264%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traditional_Welsh_dance_and_dress,_Senedd,_St_David%27s_Day_2009_Gwisgoedd_a_dawnsio_traddodiadol_Gymreig,_Senedd,_Dydd_G%C5%B5yl_Dewi_2009_(3678719264).jpg)

Welsh dancer at the Senedd building

Traditional dances include Welsh [folk dancing](/wiki/Welsh_dance "Welsh dance") and [clog dancing](/wiki/Welsh_stepdance "Welsh stepdance"). The first mention of dancing in Wales is in a 12th-century account by [Giraldus Cambrensis](/wiki/Giraldus_Cambrensis "Giraldus Cambrensis"), but by the 19th century traditional dance had all but died out due to religious opposition.[[270]](#cite_note-Davies192-274) In the 20th century a revival was led by [Lois Blake](/wiki/Lois_Blake "Lois Blake") (1890–1974).[[270]](#cite_note-Davies192-274) Clog dancing was preserved and developed by Hywel Wood (1882–1967) and others who perpetuated the art on local and national stages.[[275]](#cite_note-Davies193-279) The Welsh Folk Dance Society was founded in 1949.[[275]](#cite_note-Davies193-279) [Contemporary dance](/wiki/Contemporary_dance "Contemporary dance") grew out of Cardiff in the 1970s.[[275]](#cite_note-Davies193-279) The [National Dance Company Wales](/wiki/National_Dance_Company_Wales "National Dance Company Wales"), formed in 1983, is now resident at the Wales Millennium Centre.[[276]](#cite_note-280)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Welsh cuisine](/wiki/Welsh_cuisine "Welsh cuisine")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Cawl_Cymreig.jpg/250px-Cawl_Cymreig.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cawl_Cymreig.jpg)

[Cawl](/wiki/Cawl "Cawl"), a traditional meat and vegetable dish from Wales.

Traditional Welsh dishes include [laverbread](/wiki/Laverbread "Laverbread") (made from *[Porphyra umbilicalis](/wiki/Porphyra_umbilicalis "Porphyra umbilicalis")*, an edible [seaweed](/wiki/Seaweed "Seaweed")), [bara brith](/wiki/Bara_brith "Bara brith") (fruit bread), [cawl](/wiki/Cawl "Cawl") (a lamb stew), [cawl cennin](/wiki/Cawl_cennin "Cawl cennin") ([leek soup](/wiki/Leek_soup "Leek soup")), and [Welsh cakes](/wiki/Welsh_cake "Welsh cake").[[277]](#cite_note-281) [Cockles](/wiki/Cockle_(bivalve) "Cockle (bivalve)") are sometimes served as a traditional breakfast with bacon and laverbread.[[278]](#cite_note-282) Although Wales has its own traditional food and has absorbed much of the cuisine of England, Welsh diets now owe more to the countries of [India](/wiki/Indian_cuisine "Indian cuisine"), [China](/wiki/Chinese_cuisine "Chinese cuisine") and the [United States](/wiki/Cuisine_of_the_United_States "Cuisine of the United States"). [Chicken tikka masala](/wiki/Chicken_tikka_masala "Chicken tikka masala") is the country's favourite dish, while hamburgers and Chinese food outsell [fish and chips](/wiki/Fish_and_chips "Fish and chips") as takeaways.[[279]](#cite_note-Davies293-283)

### Sport

Main article: [Sport in Wales](/wiki/Sport_in_Wales "Sport in Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Millennium_Stadium_%28aerial_view%29.jpg/250px-Millennium_Stadium_%28aerial_view%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Millennium_Stadium_(aerial_view).jpg)

[Millennium Stadium](/wiki/Millennium_Stadium "Millennium Stadium"), Cardiff

More than 50 [national governing bodies](/wiki/Governing_bodies_of_sports_in_Wales "Governing bodies of sports in Wales") regulate and organise their sports in Wales.[[280]](#cite_note-284) Most of those involved in competitive sports select, organise and manage individuals or teams to represent their country at international events or fixtures against other countries. Wales is represented at major world sporting events such as the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup"), [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"), [Rugby League World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_League_World_Cup "Rugby League World Cup") and the [Commonwealth Games](/wiki/Commonwealth_Games "Commonwealth Games"). At the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games"), Welsh athletes compete alongside those of Scotland, England and Northern Ireland as part of a [Great Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain_at_the_Olympics "Great Britain at the Olympics") team. Wales has hosted several international sporting events.[[281]](#cite_note-walessportsevents-285) These include the [1958 Commonwealth Games](/wiki/1958_Commonwealth_Games "1958 Commonwealth Games"),[[282]](#cite_note-286) the [1999 Rugby World Cup](/wiki/1999_Rugby_World_Cup "1999 Rugby World Cup"), the [2010 Ryder Cup](/wiki/2010_Ryder_Cup "2010 Ryder Cup") and the [2017 UEFA Champions League Final](/wiki/2017_UEFA_Champions_League_Final "2017 UEFA Champions League Final").[[281]](#cite_note-walessportsevents-285)[[283]](#cite_note-287)

Although football has traditionally been the more popular sport in [North Wales](/wiki/North_Wales "North Wales"), [rugby union](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Wales "Rugby union in Wales") is seen as a symbol of [Welsh identity](/wiki/Welsh_identity "Welsh identity") and an expression of national consciousness.[[284]](#cite_note-288) The [Wales national rugby union team](/wiki/Wales_national_rugby_union_team "Wales national rugby union team") takes part in the annual [Six Nations Championship](/wiki/Six_Nations_Championship "Six Nations Championship") and has also competed in every [Rugby World Cup](/wiki/Rugby_World_Cup "Rugby World Cup"), hosting the tournament in [1999](/wiki/1999_Rugby_World_Cup "1999 Rugby World Cup"). The five professional sides that replaced the traditional club sides in major competitions in 2003 were replaced in 2004 by the four regions: [Cardiff Blues](/wiki/Cardiff_Blues "Cardiff Blues"), [Dragons](/wiki/Dragons_(rugby_union) "Dragons (rugby union)"), [Ospreys](/wiki/Ospreys_(rugby_union) "Ospreys (rugby union)") and [Scarlets](/wiki/Scarlets "Scarlets").[[285]](#cite_note-289) The Welsh regional teams play in the [United Rugby Championship](/wiki/United_Rugby_Championship "United Rugby Championship"),[[286]](#cite_note-290) the [Heineken Champions Cup](/wiki/Heineken_Champions_Cup "Heineken Champions Cup") if they qualify[[287]](#cite_note-291) and the [European Rugby Challenge Cup](/wiki/European_Rugby_Challenge_Cup "European Rugby Challenge Cup"), again dependent on qualification.[[288]](#cite_note-292) [Rugby league in Wales](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_Wales "Rugby league in Wales") dates back to 1907. A professional [Welsh League](/wiki/Welsh_League "Welsh League") existed from 1908 to 1910.[[289]](#cite_note-293)

Wales has had [its own football](/wiki/Football_in_Wales "Football in Wales") league, the [Welsh Premier League](/wiki/Welsh_Premier_League "Welsh Premier League"), since 1992.[[290]](#cite_note-294) For historical reasons, five Welsh clubs play in the [English football league system](/wiki/English_football_league_system "English football league system"): [Cardiff City](/wiki/Cardiff_City "Cardiff City"), [Swansea City](/wiki/Swansea_City "Swansea City"), [Newport County](/wiki/Newport_County_A.F.C "Newport County A.F.C"), [Wrexham](/wiki/Wrexham_A.F.C. "Wrexham A.F.C."), and [Merthyr Town](/wiki/Merthyr_Town "Merthyr Town").[[291]](#cite_note-295) The country has produced a considerable number of [footballers](/wiki/List_of_Wales_international_footballers "List of Wales international footballers") who have played at international level.[[292]](#cite_note-296) At [UEFA Euro 2016](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2016 "UEFA Euro 2016"), the [Wales national team](/wiki/Wales_national_football_team "Wales national football team") achieved their best ever finish, reaching the semi-finals.[[293]](#cite_note-297)

In international [cricket](/wiki/Cricket "Cricket"), Wales and England field a single representative team, administered by the [England and Wales Cricket Board](/wiki/England_and_Wales_Cricket_Board "England and Wales Cricket Board") (ECB), called the [England cricket team](/wiki/England_cricket_team "England cricket team"), or simply 'England'.[[294]](#cite_note-298) Occasionally, a separate [Wales team](/wiki/Wales_national_cricket_team "Wales national cricket team") play limited-overs competitions. [Glamorgan County Cricket Club](/wiki/Glamorgan_County_Cricket_Club "Glamorgan County Cricket Club") is the only Welsh participant in the England and Wales County Championship.[[295]](#cite_note-299) Wales has produced notable participants of individual sports including [snooker](/wiki/Snooker "Snooker"),[[296]](#cite_note-300) track and field,[[297]](#cite_note-301) [cycling](/wiki/Cycle_sport "Cycle sport"),[[298]](#cite_note-302)[[299]](#cite_note-303) and [boxing](/wiki/List_of_Welsh_boxing_champions "List of Welsh boxing champions").[[300]](#cite_note-304)[[301]](#cite_note-305)

### National symbols and identity

Main articles: [Welsh national identity](/wiki/Welsh_national_identity "Welsh national identity") and [National symbols of Wales](/wiki/National_symbols_of_Wales "National symbols of Wales")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/Welsh_Dragon_%28Y_Ddraig_Goch%29.svg/250px-Welsh_Dragon_%28Y_Ddraig_Goch%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Welsh_Dragon_(Y_Ddraig_Goch).svg)

The [red dragon](/wiki/Welsh_Dragon "Welsh Dragon"), a popular symbol in Wales.

Wales is regarded as a modern [Celtic nation](/wiki/Celtic_nation "Celtic nation") which contributes to its national identity,[[302]](#cite_note-Museum_Wales-2022-306)[[303]](#cite_note-kochnation-307) with Welsh artists regularly appearing at [Celtic festivals](/wiki/Celtic_festivals "Celtic festivals").[[304]](#cite_note-308) The [red dragon](/wiki/Welsh_Dragon "Welsh Dragon") is the principal symbol of national identity and pride, personifying the fearlessness of the Welsh nation.[[75]](#cite_note-Wales-2019-79) The dragon is first referenced in literature as a symbol of the people in the [Historia Brittonum](/wiki/Historia_Brittonum "Historia Brittonum"). [Vortigern](/wiki/Vortigern "Vortigern") ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language"): *Gwrtheyrn*), [King of the Celtic Britons](/wiki/King_of_the_Britons "King of the Britons"), is interrupted while attempting to build a fort at Dinas Emrys. He is told by Ambrosius[[e]](#cite_note-310) to dig up two dragons beneath the castle. He discovers a red dragon representing the [Celtic Britons](/wiki/Celtic_Britons "Celtic Britons"), and a white dragon representing Anglo-Saxons. Ambrosius prophesies that the Celtic Britons will reclaim the island and push the Anglo-Saxons back to the sea.[[306]](#cite_note-HistoriaBrittonum-311)

As an emblem, the red dragon of Wales has been used since the reign of [Cadwaladr](/wiki/Cadwaladr "Cadwaladr"), [King of Gwynedd](/wiki/List_of_rulers_of_Wales "List of rulers of Wales") from around 655 AD, and appears prominently on the national [flag of Wales](/wiki/Flag_of_Wales "Flag of Wales"), which became an official flag in 1959.[[307]](#cite_note-312) The banner of [Owain Glyndŵr](/wiki/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r "Owain Glyndŵr") is associated with Welsh nationhood; it was carried into battle by Welsh forces during [Glyndŵr's battles](/wiki/Glynd%C5%B5r_Rising "Glyndŵr Rising") against the English, and includes four lions on red and gold.[[308]](#cite_note-313) The standard is similar to the arms of [Llywelyn ap Gruffudd](/wiki/Llywelyn_ap_Gruffudd "Llywelyn ap Gruffudd") (Llywelyn the Last), the last Prince of Wales before the conquest of Wales by [Edward I of England](/wiki/Edward_I_of_England "Edward I of England"). The design may also be influenced by the arms of Glyndŵr's parents, both of whom had lions in their arms. [Owain Glyndŵr Day](/wiki/Owain_Glynd%C5%B5r_Day "Owain Glyndŵr Day") is celebrated on 16 September in Wales and there have been calls to make it a national bank holiday.[[309]](#cite_note-314)[[310]](#cite_note-BBC-2022-315)[[311]](#cite_note-316) The [Prince of Wales's feathers](/wiki/Prince_of_Wales%27s_feathers "Prince of Wales's feathers") is also used in Wales: it consists of three white feathers emerging from a gold coronet, and the German motto *Ich dien* (I serve). Several Welsh representative teams, including [the Welsh rugby union](/wiki/Logo_of_the_Welsh_Rugby_Union "Logo of the Welsh Rugby Union"), and Welsh regiments in the [British Army](/wiki/British_Army "British Army"), including the [Royal Welsh](/wiki/Royal_Welsh "Royal Welsh"), use the badge or a stylised version of it.[[312]](#cite_note-317)[[313]](#cite_note-318)[[f]](#cite_note-320)

On 1 March, Welsh people celebrate [Saint David's Day](/wiki/Saint_David%27s_Day "Saint David's Day"), commemorating the death of the country's patron saint in 589.[[315]](#cite_note-321) It is not a recognised [bank holiday](/wiki/Bank_holiday "Bank holiday") although there have been calls to make it so.[[316]](#cite_note-Mosalski-2022-322)[[317]](#cite_note-BBC-2006-323)[[318]](#cite_note-YouGov-2022-324) The day is celebrated by schools and cultural societies across Wales, and customs include the wearing of a [leek](/wiki/Leek "Leek") or a [daffodil](/wiki/Daffodil "Daffodil"), which are two national emblems of Wales. Children also wear the national costume.[[319]](#cite_note-325) The origins of the leek can be traced to the 16th century, while the daffodil became popular in the 19th century, encouraged by [David Lloyd George](/wiki/David_Lloyd_George "David Lloyd George").[[320]](#cite_note-Davies189-326) This is attributed to confusion (or association) between the Welsh for leeks, *cennin*, and that for daffodils, *cennin Pedr* or St. Peter's leeks.[[52]](#cite_note-ONS_Cymru-56) A report in 1916 gave preference to the leek, which has appeared on British pound coins.[[320]](#cite_note-Davies189-326) Other Welsh festivals include [Mabsant](/wiki/Mabsant "Mabsant") when parishes would celebrate the patron saint of their local church, although this is now rarely observed,[[321]](#cite_note-327) and a more modern celebration, [Dydd Santes Dwynwen](/wiki/Dydd_Santes_Dwynwen "Dydd Santes Dwynwen") (St Dwynwen's Day), observed on 25 January in a similar way to St Valentine's Day.[[322]](#cite_note-328)

*[Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau](/wiki/Hen_Wlad_Fy_Nhadau "Hen Wlad Fy Nhadau")* ([Welsh](/wiki/Welsh_language "Welsh language") for 'land of my fathers') is the *de facto*, national anthem of Wales and is played at events such as football or rugby matches involving the Wales national team, as well as the opening of the Senedd and other official occasions.[[323]](#cite_note-329) "*Cymru am byth*" ('Wales forever') is a popular Welsh motto.[[324]](#cite_note-330) Another Welsh motto "*Y [Ddraig Goch](/wiki/Welsh_Dragon "Welsh Dragon") Ddyry Cychwyn*" ('the [red dragon](/wiki/Welsh_Dragon "Welsh Dragon") inspires action') has been used on the [Royal Badge of Wales](/wiki/Royal_Badge_of_Wales "Royal Badge of Wales") when it was created in 1953.[[325]](#cite_note-331)

## Notes

1. **[^](#cite_ref-7)** Date powers transferred to National Assembly
2. **[^](#cite_ref-9)** ONS Standard Area Measurement, 'area to mean high water excluding inland water'
3. **[^](#cite_ref-10)** [ONS](/wiki/Office_for_National_Statistics "Office for National Statistics") Standard Area Measurement, 'total extent of the realm' (area to mean low water)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-16)** Both .wales and .cymru are not [ccTLDs](/wiki/CcTLDs "CcTLDs"), but [GeoTLDs](/wiki/Geographic_top-level_domain "Geographic top-level domain"), open to use by all people in Wales and related to Wales. [.uk](/wiki/.uk ".uk") as part of the United Kingdom is also used. [ISO 3166-1](/wiki/ISO_3166-1 "ISO 3166-1") is [GB](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain"), but [.gb](/wiki/.gb ".gb") is unused.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-310)** "[Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Ambrosius vocor, id est, Embreis Guletic.*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") '"I am called Ambrosius, that is Embreis Guletic"'.[[305]](#cite_note-309) Embreis Guletic is probably Emrys Gwledig.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-320)** Wales is not separately represented on the [Union Jack](/wiki/Union_Jack "Union Jack") as, at the time of the flag's creation, Wales was considered part of England.[[314]](#cite_note-319)
